nodes.title: Brouwer fixed point theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let \(f : Dn \to Dn\) be a continuous map from the (unit) disk in \(\mathbb{R}n to itself\). Then \(f\) has a fixed point, i.e., there is some point \(x \in Dn\) such that \(f\) (\(x\)) = \(x\).

For \(n = 1\) this is a well-known result from calculus: The graph of any continuous map \(f : [0, 1] \to [0, 1]\) must cross the diagonal \(y = x\) for some \(x\)∗ ∈ [0, 1]. Hence, \(f\) (\(x\)∗ ) = \(x\)∗ . See Figure 1.4. The second theorem is the fundamental theorem of algebra.

nodes.title: The fundamental theorem of algebra
nodes.originalText:
A polynomial equation

\[zn + an-1 zn-1 + \cdots + a1 z + a0 = 0\]

of degree \(n > 0\) with complex coefficients has at least one complex root.

Chapter 1. Introduction 7

\(y\) (1, 1) ∙

\[y=x y = f (x) x\]

\(x\)∗ \(x\)

Figure 1.4: The graph of any continuous map from [0, 1] to [0, 1] must cross the diagonal.

To prove it we will use the fact that the fundamental group of the circle is isomorphic to the group of integers. The fundamental theorem of algebra may be proved in many different ways, including using only algebraic techniques and analysis. However, the proof we will provide (based on [4, §56]) is a fairly simple corollary of the computation of the fundamental group of the circle.

2. Continuous maps

2.1 Metric spaces From calculus we know what to mean by a continuous map from \(\mathbb{R}n to \mathbb{R}m : a\) map \(f : \mathbb{R}n \to \mathbb{R}m\) is continuous at \(p \in \mathbb{R}n\) if for all \(\epsilon > 0\) there exists a \(\delta > 0\) such that if ‖\(p - q\|\mathbb{R}n < \delta, then\) ‖\(f\) (\(p\)) − \(f\) (\(q\))‖\(\mathbb{R}m < \epsilon\). Here ‖ ⋅ ‖\(\mathbb{R}n\) denotes the Euclidean norm in \(\mathbb{R}n\) . Similarly, ‖ ⋅ ‖\(\mathbb{R}m\) denotes the Euclidean norm in \(\mathbb{R}m\) . Topological spaces provide the most general setting for which the concept of continuity makes sense. Before we get to the concept of a topological space, let us consider metric spaces. Metric spaces allow us to speak of distance between elements. Using the notion of distance between elements we can make sense of continuity of maps between metric spaces.

nodes.title: Metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
A metric space (\(X, d)\) is a non-empty set \(X together\) with a map \(d : X \times X \to \mathbb{R}\) called a metric such that the following properties hold:

M1 \(d\)(\(x, y) \ge 0\) for all \(x, y \in X, and\) \(d\)(\(x, y) = 0\) if and only if \(x = y\);

M2 \(d\)(\(x, y) = d(y, x)\) for all \(x, y \in X\);

M3 \(d\)(\(x, z) \le d(x, y) + d(y, z)\) for all \(x, y, z \in X\).

The first condition says that the distance between two elements is always positive, and equal to zero if and only if the two elements are the same. The second condition says that distance is symmetric. The third condition says that the triangle inequality holds. The metric \(d\) is sometimes also referred to as a distance function.

2.2 (\(\mathbb{R}n\) seen as a metric space) Let \(X = \mathbb{R}\) and \(d\) be the map defined by \(d\)(\(x, y) = Example\) √ |\(x - y|(= (x - y)2\) ). The first two requirements for \(d\) are clearly satisfied, and the third follows from the usual triangle inequality for real numbers,

\[d(x, z) = |x - z| = |(x - y) + (y - z)| \le |x - y| + |y - z| = d(x, y) + d(y, z).\]

For \(X = \mathbb{R}n\) with \(n > 0\) an integer, √ let \(d\)(\(x, y) = \|x - y\|\) where ‖ ⋅ ‖ is the Euclidean norm, e.g., for \(n = 2, d(x, y) = \|x - y\| = (x1 - y1\) )2 + (\(x2 - y2\) )2 . Again, the first two requirements for \(d\) are clearly satisfied. The third requirement follows from the triangle inequality for vectors in \(\mathbb{R}n\) .

We may equip \(\mathbb{R}n\) with other metrics than the one described in Example 2.2. For instance, for \(X = \mathbb{R}2 , let\) \(d\)(\(x, y) = |x1 - y1\) | + |\(x2 - y2\) |.

This is known as the taxi cab metric.

Chapter 2. Continuous maps 9

We say that two metrics \(d1\) and \(d2\) on the same set \(X\) are equivalent if there are constants \(L\) and \(M\) such that \(d1\) (\(x, y) \le Ld2\) (\(x, y)\) and \(d2\) (\(x, y) \le Md1\) (\(x, y)\)

for all \(x, y \in X\).

nodes.title: Discrete metric spaces
nodes.originalText: For any set \(X, let\) \(d : X \times X \to \mathbb{R}\) be the map given by { 1 \(x \ne y, d(x, y) = 0\) \(x = y\). We call \(d\) the discrete metric on \(X\).

nodes.title: \(C\)[\(a, b]\)
nodes.originalText: Let \(X = C[a, b], i\).e., the set of continuous maps from the interval \(I = [a, b] \subseteq \mathbb{R} to \mathbb{R}, and\) let \(d\)(\(x, y) = max\) |\(x\)(\(i\)) − \(y\)(\(i\))|. \(i\inI\)

nodes.title: Subspaces of metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
If \(d\) is a metric on a set \(X, and\) \(A \subseteq X\) is any subset of \(X, then\) \(d\) is also a metric on \(A\).

2.2 Continuous maps between metric spaces The definition of continuity of maps between metric spaces is completely analogous to the situation that we have from calculus.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, dX\) ) and (\(Y , dY\) ) be two metric spaces. A map \(f : X \to Y\) is continuous at \(p \in X\) if for all \(\epsilon > 0\) there is a \(\delta > 0\) such that if \(dX\) (\(p, q) < \delta\) then \(dY\) (\(f\) (\(p\)), \(f\) (\(q\))) < \(\epsilon\). If \(f\) is continuous at every \(p \in X, we\) say that \(f\) is continuous.

To get us to the setting of topological spaces we will need the concept of open and closed sets.

nodes.title: Open and closed balls
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space, and let \(a \in X\) and \(r > 0\) be real number. The open ball centered at \(a\) with radius \(r\) is the subset

\[B(a; r) = {x \in X \mid d(x, a) < r}\]

of \(X\). The closed ball centered at \(a\) with radius \(r\) is the subset

\[B(a; r) = {x \in X \mid d(x, a) \le r}\]

of \(X\).

In Euclidean space with the usual metric (induced from Euclidean norm), a ball (as defined above) is precisely what we think of as a ball in everyday language. Open balls are sometimes referred to as simply balls, and closed balls are sometimes referred to as discs, e.g. Theorem 1.1.

10 2.2. Continuous maps between metric spaces

nodes.title: Open balls in discrete metric spaces
nodes.originalText: Let (\(X, d)\) be the metric space defined in Example 2.3. Then \(B\)(\(x\); \(r1\) ) = {\(x\)} and \(B\)(\(x\); \(r2\) ) = \(X\) for all 0 < \(r1 \le 1\) and all \(r2 > 1\).

nodes.title: Open and closed sets
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space. A subset \(A \subseteq X\) is open in \(X\) if for every point \(a \in A, there\) is an open ball \(B\)(\(a\); \(r\)) about \(a\) contained in \(A\). We say that \(A\) is closed in \(X\) if the complement \(Ac = X\) ⧵ \(A = {x \in X\) ∣ \(x \notin A}\) is open.

Most subsets are neither open nor closed. Subsets that are both open and closed are sometimes referred to as clopen. In particular, both \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) are clopen in \(X\).

nodes.title: Open balls are open and closed balls are closed
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space, \(x \in X\) and \(r > 0\) a real number. Then the open ball \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) ⊆ \(X\) is open in \(X, and\) the closed ball \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) ⊆ \(X\) is closed in \(X\).

Proof. We prove the statement about open balls. The statement about closed balls follows from a similar argument. Assume that \(y \in B(x\); \(r\)). We need to prove that there is an open ball \(B\)(\(y\); \(\epsilon\)) about \(y\) that is contained in \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)). Let \(\epsilon = r - d(x, y)\). By the triangle inequality of the metric \(d, M3, we\) have that for \(z \in B(y\); \(\epsilon\)),

\[d(x, z) \le d(x, y) + d(y, z) < d(x, y) + \epsilon = d(x, y) + r - d(x, y) = r.\]

\[Hence, B(y; \epsilon) \subseteq B(x; r).\]

For a metric space (\(X, d), a\) subset \(A \subseteq X\) and \(x \in X, we\) say that: (i) \(x\) is an interior point of \(A\) if there is an open ball \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) about \(x\) which is contained in \(A, (ii)\) \(x\) is an exterior point of \(A\) if there is an open ball \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) which is contained in \(Ac\) and (iii) \(x\) is a boundary point if all open balls about \(x\) contains points in \(A\) and in \(Ac\) . Hence, \(A\) is open in \(X\) if and only if \(A\) only consists of its interior points. An interior point will always belong to \(A\). An exterior point will never belong to \(A\). A boundary point will some times belong to \(A, and\) some times to \(Ac\) .

nodes.title: Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText: Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space, \(A\) a subset of \(X\) and \(x \in X\). We say that \(A\) is a neighborhood of \(x\) if there is an open ball about \(x\) that is contained in \(A\). We say that \(A\) is an open neighborhood (of \(x\)) if \(A\) itself is open.

nodes.title: Continuity at a point
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, dX\) ) and (\(Y , dY\) ) be two metric spaces, and let \(p \in X\). A map \(f : X \to Y\) is continuous at \(p\) if and only if for all neighborhoods \(B\) of \(f\) (\(p\)), there is a neighborhood \(A\) of \(p\) such that \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(B\).

Chapter 2. Continuous maps 11

Proof. Assume that \(f\) is continuous at \(p\). If \(B\) is a neighborhood of \(f\) (\(p\)), then, by definition, there is an open ball \(BY\) (\(f\) (\(p\)); \(\epsilon\)) about \(f\) (\(p\)) that is contained in \(B\). Since \(f\) is continuous at \(p, there\) is a \(\delta > 0\) such that if \(dX\) (\(p, q) < \delta, then\) \(dY\) (\(f\) (\(p\)), \(f\) (\(q\))) < \(\epsilon\). Hence, \(f\) (\(BX\) (\(p\); \(\delta\))) ⊆ \(BY\) (\(f\) (\(p\)); \(\epsilon\)) ⊆ \(B\). That is, if we let \(A = BX\) (\(p, \delta), then\) for all neighborhoods \(B\) of \(f\) (\(p\)), we have that \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(B\) where \(A\) is a neighborhood of \(p\). Assume that for all neighborhoods \(B\) of \(f\) (\(p\)), there is a neighborhood \(A\) of \(p\) such that \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(B\). We need to prove that for all \(\epsilon > 0, there\) is a \(\delta > 0\) such that if \(dX\) (\(p, q) < \delta, then\) \(dY\) (\(f\) (\(p\)), \(f\) (\(q\))) < \(\epsilon\). By utilizing the fact that \(B = BY\) (\(f\) (\(p\)); \(\epsilon\)) is a neighborhood of \(f\) (\(p\)), then, by assumption, there must be a neighborhood \(A\) of \(p\) such that \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(B\). Since \(A\) is a neighborhood of \(p, there\) is an open ball \(BX\) (\(p\); \(\delta\)) about \(p\) that is contained in \(A\). Now assume that \(dX\) (\(p, p\)′ ) < \(\delta\). Then \(p\)′ ∈ \(BX\) (\(p\); \(\delta\)) ⊆ \(A\). Thus \(f\) (\(p\)′ ) ∈ \(B = BY\) (\(f\) (\(p\)); \(\epsilon\)), and hence, \(dY\) (\(f\) (\(p\)), \(f\) (\(p\)′ )) < \(\epsilon\). Thus \(f\) is continuous at \(p\).

The following theorem gives an alternative description of continuous maps between metric spaces.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, dX\) ) and (\(Y , dY\) ) be two metric spaces. A map \(f : X \to Y\) is continuous if and only if for every subset \(B \subseteq Y\) open in \(Y , the\) preimage of \(B\) under \(f\) ,

\[f -1 (B) = {x \in X \mid f (x) \in B} \subseteq X,\]

is open in \(X\).

Proof. Assume that \(f\) is continuous. For \(B \subseteq Y\) open in \(Y , we\) have to prove that \(f -1\) (\(B\)) ⊆ \(X\) is open in \(X\). Let \(a \in f -1\) (\(B\)). We want to prove that there is an open ball about \(a\) in \(X\) that is contained in \(f -1\) (\(B\)). By assumption, \(B \subseteq Y\) is open in \(Y\) . Hence, there is an \(\epsilon > 0\) such that \(BY\) (\(f\) (\(a\)); \(\epsilon\)) ⊆ \(B\). From the assumption that \(f\) is continuous there is a \(\delta > 0\) such that \(BX\) (\(a\); \(\delta\)) ⊆ \(f -1\) (\(BY\) (\(f\) (\(a\)); \(\epsilon\))) ⊆ \(f -1\) (\(B\)). We now prove the opposite implication. Assume that for every subset \(B \subseteq Y\) open in \(Y , the\) preimage \(f -1\) (\(B\)) of \(B\) under \(f\) is open in \(X\). Let \(a \in X\) and \(\epsilon > 0\) be a real number. From the first assumption it follows that \(f -1\) (\(BY\) (\(f\) (\(a\)); \(\epsilon\))) ⊆ \(X\) is open in \(X\). As \(f -1\) (\(BY\) (\(f\) (\(a\)); \(\epsilon\))) is open and contains \(a, there\) is a \(\delta > 0\) such that \(BX\) (\(a\); \(\delta\)) ⊆ \(f -1\) (\(BY\) (\(f\) (\(a\)); \(\epsilon\))). Thus \(x \in BX\) (\(a\); \(\delta\)) implies that \(f\) (\(x\)) ∈ \(BY\) (\(f\) (\(a\)); \(\epsilon\)). Hence, \(f : X \to Y\) is continuous.

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sets, and let \(f : A \to B\). Then \(f -1\) (\(B\)) will always exist even if there is no inverse map. In the cases where \(f\) has an inverse there is no ambiguity. If \(U\) and \(V\) are both subsets of \(B\) then

\(f -1\) (\(U\) ∪ \(V\) ) = \(f -1\) (\(U\) ) ∪ \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) and \(f -1\) (\(U\) ∩ \(V\) ) = \(f -1\) (\(U\) ) ∩ \(f -1\) (\(V\) ),

and furthermore, if \(U \subseteq V\) then \(f -1\) (\(U\) ) ⊆ \(f -1\) (\(V\) ). Let \(U \subseteq A\) and \(V \subseteq B, then\) ( ) \(U \subseteq f -1\) (\(f\) (\(U\) )) and \(f\) \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) ⊆ \(V\) .

We also note that if \(U\) is a subset of \(B\) then

\[f -1 (B \setminus U ) = f -1 (U c ) = (f -1 (U ))c = A \setminus f -1 (U ).\]

12 2.3. Exercises

2.3 Exercises

\[Exercise 2.1 Does d(x, y) = (x - y)2 define a metric on X = \mathbb{R}?\]

Exercise 2.2 Show that \(\mathbb{R}2\) equipped with the taxi cab metric is a metric space.

Exercise 2.3 Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space. Show that the map \(d\) ′ ∶ \(X \times X \to \mathbb{R}\) given by

\(d\)(\(x, y)\) \(d\) ′ (\(x, y) = 1 + d(x, y)\) is also a metric on \(X\).

Exercise 2.4 Draw a picture of the open ball \(B\)((0, 0); 1) in the metric space (\(\mathbb{R}2 , d)\) with

\[(a) d(x, y) = d1 (x, y) = |x1 - y1 | + |x2 - y2 |; \sqrt{(b)} d(x, y) = d2 (x, y) = (x1 - y1 )2 + (x2 - y2 )2 ;\]

\[(c) d(x, y) = d\infty (x, y) = max{|x1 - y1 |, |x2 - y2 |}.\]

Exercise 2.5 Show that \(d1 , d2\) and \(d\)∞ (as defined in Exercise 2.4) are equivalent on \(X = \mathbb{R}2\) .

Exercise 2.6 Show that in a discrete metric space (\(X, d), cf\). Example 2.3, every subset is both open and closed in \(X\).

Exercise 2.7 Show that for equivalent metrics \(d\) and \(d\) ′ on the set \(X, the\) open sets are the same.

Exercise 2.8 Let (\(X, dX\) ) and (\(Y , dY\) ) be metric spaces, and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a map. Show that \(f\) is continuous if and only if for every subset \(B \subseteq Y\) closed in \(Y , the\) preimage \(f -1\) (\(B\)) is closed in \(X\).

3. Topological spaces

3.1 Definition and examples Topological spaces are spaces constructed to support continuous maps. The definition is as follows.

nodes.title: Topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
A topological space is a set \(X together\) with a collection \(\mathcal{T}\) of subsets of \(X\) that are called open in \(X, such\) that the following properties hold.

T1 The subsets \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) are in \(\mathcal{T}\) .

T2 The union of the elements of any subcollection of \(\mathcal{T}\) is in \(\mathcal{T}\) .

T3 The intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of \(\mathcal{T}\) is in \(\mathcal{T}\) .

A topological space is strictly speaking an ordered pair (\(X, \mathcal{T}\) ). We refer to \(\mathcal{T}\) as the topology on \(X\). We will often omit specific mention of \(\mathcal{T}\) if no confusion will arise.

The following theorem states that every metric space (\(X, d)\) is a topological space with the metric topology \(\mathcal{T}d\) on \(X\).

nodes.title: Metric spaces are topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space. Let \(\mathcal{T}d\) be the collection of subsets \(U \subseteq X\) with the property that \(U \in \mathcal{T}d\) if and only if for each \(x \in U\) there is an \(r > 0\) such that \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) ⊆ \(U\) . Then \(\mathcal{T}d\) defines a topology on \(X\).

Proof. Clearly, \(\emptyset \in \mathcal{T}d\) . To show that \(X \in \mathcal{T}d , note\) that for any \(x \in X, B(x\); 1) ⊆ \(X\). Hence, \(X \in \mathcal{T}d\) . Thus T1 is satisfied. ⋃ Let {\(U\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be any subcollection of \(\mathcal{T}d\) . We need to prove that \(V = \lambda\)∈Λ \(U\lambda \in \mathcal{T}d\) . Let \(x \in V\) . ⋃ From \(V = \lambda\)∈Λ \(U\lambda\) there is \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ such that \(x \in U\lambda\) . By the property of \(U\lambda\) satisfied by the \(U\lambda\) in \(\mathcal{T}d\) there is an \(r > 0\) such that \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) ⊆ \(U\lambda\) . Hence, \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) ⊆ \(V\) . Thus \(V \in \mathcal{T}d , and\) T2 is satisfied. We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of \(\mathcal{T}d\) is also an element of \(\mathcal{T}d\) . Let \(U1 , U2\) , … , \(Un \in \mathcal{T}d\) . We need to prove that \(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Un \in \mathcal{T}d\) . Let \(x \in U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Un\) . Since \(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Un \subseteq Ui , we\) have that \(x \in Ui\) for \(i = 1, 2\), … , \(n\). By the defining property of \(\mathcal{T}d\) there is an \(ri > 0\) such that \(B\)(\(x\); \(ri\) ) ⊆ \(Ui\) for \(i = 1, 2\), … , \(n\). Let \(r = min{r1 , r2\) , … , \(rn\) }. Then \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) ⊆ \(B\)(\(x\); \(ri\) ) ⊆ \(Ui\) for \(i = 1, 2\), … , \(n\). Thus \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) ⊆ \(U1\) ∩\(U2\) ∩⋯∩\(Un , and\) so \(U1\) ∩\(U2\) ∩⋯∩\(Un \in \mathcal{T}d\) . Hence, T3 is satisfied.

The following theorem relates the metric topologies for two equivalent metrics.

nodes.title: Equivalent metrics induce the same topology
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be any set, and let \(d1\) and \(d2\) be two equivalent metrics on \(X\). Then \(\mathcal{T}d1 = \mathcal{T}d2\) .

14 3.1. Definition and examples

This follows from Exercise 2.7.

nodes.title: Discrete topology
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be any set. The collection \(\mathcal{T}\) of all subsets of \(X, i\).e. the power set \(\mathcal{P}\)(\(X\)) of \(X, is\) a topology on \(X\). We refer to this topology as the discrete topology. A set \(X\) equipped with the discrete topology is referred to as a discrete topological space.

The discrete topology is the unique topology where the singletons are open. We can think of a discrete topological space as a space of separate, isolated points, with no close interaction between different points. For any set \(X, the\) discrete topology is the largest topology we may equip \(X\) with. The smallest topology is called the indiscrete topology.

nodes.title: Indiscrete topology
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be any set. The collection \(\mathcal{T}\) consisting of \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) is a topology on \(X, referred to as\) the indiscrete topology on \(X\). A set \(X\) equipped with the indiscrete topology is referred to as an indiscrete topological space.

nodes.title: Examples of topologies on a three-point set
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X = {a, b, c}\). The following collections all define a topology on \(X\).

\[(1) \mathcal{T}1 = \mathcal{T}ind = {\emptyset, X}\]

\[(2) \mathcal{T}2 = {\emptyset, {a}, X}\]

\[(3) \mathcal{T}3 = {\emptyset, {a, b}, X}\]

\[(4) \mathcal{T}4 = {\emptyset, {a}, {a, b}, X}\]

\[(5) \mathcal{T}5 = {\emptyset, {a, b}, {b}, {b, c}, X}\]

\[(6) \mathcal{T}6 = \mathcal{T}disc = \mathcal{P}(X) = {\emptyset, {a}, {a, b}, {a, c}, {b}, {b, c}, {c}, X}\]

There are in total 29 topologies on \(X\). However, there are also collections of subsets of \(X\) which do not define topologies on \(X\). None of the following collections of subsets of \(X\) define a topology on \(X\).

(1) {\(\emptyset, {a}, {b}, X}\)

(2) {\(\emptyset, {a}, {c}, X}\)

(3) {\(\emptyset, {a, b}, {b, c}, X}\)

nodes.title: Comparable topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be any set and suppose that \(\mathcal{T}1\) and \(\mathcal{T}2\) are two topologies on \(X\). If \(\mathcal{T}1 \subseteq \mathcal{T}2 , we\) say that \(\mathcal{T}1\) is coarser than \(\mathcal{T}2\) and that \(\mathcal{T}2\) is finer than \(\mathcal{T}1\) . We say that \(\mathcal{T}1\) and \(\mathcal{T}2\) are comparable if either \(\mathcal{T}1 \subseteq \mathcal{T}2\) or \(\mathcal{T}2 \subseteq \mathcal{T}1\) .

Clearly, for any set \(X, the\) discrete topology \(\mathcal{T}disc\) contains the indiscrete topology \(\mathcal{T}ind : \mathcal{T}disc \supseteq \mathcal{T}ind\) . Hence, the discrete topology is finer than the indiscrete topology and the indiscrete topology is coarser than the discrete topology.

nodes.title: Cofinite topology
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be any set. The collection \(\mathcal{T}\) of subsets of \(X\) consisting of subsets \(U \subseteq X\) such that \(U\) \(c = X\) ⧵ \(U\) is either finite or all of \(X\) is a topology on \(X\). We refer to this topology as the cofinite topology on \(X\).

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 15

If \(X\) is a finite set, the cofinite topology is equal to the discrete topology. If \(X\) is an infinite set, the cofinite topology is strictly coarser than the discrete topology, in the sense that the cofinite topology is properly contained in the discrete topology. We end this section with a theorem that we can use to prove that some set is open. To state the theorem we need the following definition.

nodes.title: Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(U\) a subset of \(X\) and \(x \in X\). We say that \(U\) is a neighborhood of \(x\) if \(x \in U\) and \(U\) is open in \(X\).

A neighborhood in the sense of the previous definition is sometimes referred to as an open neigh- borhood, cf. Definition 2.11.

nodes.title: Characterization of open sets by neighborhoods
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. A subset \(U\) of \(X\) is open in \(X\) if and only if for every \(x \in U\) there is a neighborhood \(Ux\) of \(x\) such that \(Ux \subseteq U\) .

Proof. Assume that \(U\) is open in \(X\). Then for every \(x \in U , U\) is a neighborhood of \(x\) that is contained in \(U\) . We prove the other implication. Assume that for every \(x \in U\) there is a \(Ux \in \mathcal{T}\) such that \(x \in Ux \subseteq U , i\).e., that \(Ux\) is a neighborhood of \(x\) such that \(Ux \subseteq U\) . To prove that \(U \in \mathcal{T} , we\) will ⋃ ⋃ prove that \(U = x\inU\) \(Ux\) . Assume that \(x\)′ ∈ \(Ux\)′ . Then \(x\)′ ∈ \(Ux\)′ ⊆ \(x\inU\) \(Ux\) . Furthermore, any ⋃ point in \(x\inU\) \(Ux\) is in \(Ux\) for some \(x \in U\) so by assumption, \(Ux \subseteq U\) and \(x \in Ux \subseteq U\) . Hence, ⋃ \(U = x\inU\) \(Ux\) . As \(U\) is the union of open sets it must be an open set as well by T2.

3.2 Continuous maps We know from Theorem 2.13 that a map between metric spaces is continuous if and only if the preimage of an open set is open. This motivates the following definition.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. A map \(f : X \to Y\) is said to be continuous if preimages of open sets are open, i.e., if \(V\) is an open set in \(Y\) then the preimage \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) of \(V\) under \(f\) is open in \(X\).

Hence, all continuous maps between metric spaces (\(X, dX\) ) and (\(Y , dY\) ) are also continuous maps between the corresponding topological spaces \(X\) and \(Y\) with the metric topologies \(\mathcal{T}dX\) and \(\mathcal{T}dY , re- spectively\).

nodes.title: Constant maps are continuous
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. Then all constant maps from \(X to Y\) are continuous: the preimages are either empty or the entire space, and these are always open, cf. T1.

nodes.title: Maps from discrete spaces are continuous
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a discrete topological space and \(Y\) a topological space. Then all maps from \(X to Y\) are continuous.

nodes.title: Maps into indiscrete spaces are continuous
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be any topological space and \(Y\) be an indiscrete topological space. Then all maps from \(X to Y\) are continuous.

16 3.2. Continuous maps

nodes.title: Identity maps between discrete and Euclidean topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}disc\) be the discrete topological space consisting of the real numbers with the discrete topology, and let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the topological space consisting of the the real numbers with the usual (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the identity map id \(\mathbb{R}disc\) ←←←←→ \(\mathbb{R}\)

is continuous by Example 3.13, while the identity map id \(\mathbb{R}\) ←←←←→ \(\mathbb{R}disc\)

is not continuous: singletons are open in the discrete topology but not in the (Euclidean) metric topology.

The following theorem says that the composition of two continuous maps is a continuous map.

nodes.title: Composition of continuous maps
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X, Y\) and \(Z\) be topological spaces. If \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) are continuous maps, then the composite \(g\)◦\(f : X \to Z\) is continuous.

Proof. Let \(W \subseteq Z\) be open in \(Z\). We need to prove that (\(g\)◦\(f\) )−1 (\(W\) ) is open in \(X\). Since

\[(g◦f )-1 (W ) = {x \in X \mid g(f (x)) \in W } = {x \in X \mid f (x) \in g -1 (W )} = {x \in X \mid x \in f -1 (g -1 (W ))} = f -1 (g -1 (W ))\]

and that \(g -1\) (\(W\) ) is open in \(Y\) and \(f -1\) (\(g -1\) (\(W\) )) is open in \(X\) (by continuity of \(g\) and \(f\) ), it follows that (\(g\)◦\(f\) )−1 (\(W\) ) is open in \(X\). Hence, \(g\)◦\(f\) is continuous.

We can express continuity at a point for maps between topological spaces using neighborhoods. (See Theorem 2.12 for the case of metric spaces.)

nodes.title: Tells us that the composition of two homeomorphisms f x y an
nodes.originalText:
tells us that the composition of two homeomorphisms \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) is continuous. The composition of two bijective maps is always bijective. Hence, \(g\)◦\(f\) is a continuous bijection. We need to prove that its inverse, (\(g\)◦\(f\) )−1 , is continuous. Since

\[(g◦f )-1 = f -1 ◦g -1\]

is a composition of continuous maps, then by Theorem 3.16 so is (\(g\)◦\(f\) )−1 . Thus \(g\)◦\(f\) is a homeomor- phism. Hence, if \(X \cong Y\) and \(Y \cong Z, then\) \(X \cong Z\). Thus ≅ satisfies the transitivity condition for an equivalence relation.

3.4 Closed sets Recall that in a topological space \(X, a\) subset \(A\) of \(X\) is an open subset if and only if \(A\) is an element of the topology of \(X, i\).e., \(A \in \mathcal{T}\) .

nodes.title: Continuity at a point
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces, and let \(x \in X\). A map \(f : X \to Y\) is continuous at \(x\) if for all neighborhoods \(V\) of \(f\) (\(x\)) there is a neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) such that \(f\) (\(U\) ) ⊆ \(V\) .

nodes.title: Continuity characterized pointwise
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. A map \(f : X \to Y\) is continuous if and only if it is continuous at each \(x \in X\).

Proof. Assume that \(f\) is continuous, and let \(x \in X\) and \(V\) be a neighborhood of \(f\) (\(x\)). Then the set \(U = f -1\) (\(V\) ) is a neighborhood of \(x\) such that \(f\) (\(U\) ) ⊆ \(V\) . Assume that \(f\) is continuous at each \(x \in X\). Let \(V \subseteq Y\) be open in \(Y\) . Choose \(x \in f -1\) (\(V\) ). Since \(f\) is continuous at \(x\) there is neighborhood \(Ux\) of \(x\) such that \(f\) (\(Ux\) ) ⊆ \(V\) . Hence, \(Ux \subseteq f -1\) (\(V\) ). It follows that \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) can be written as the union of the open sets \(Ux , and\) hence, it is open in \(X\). Thus \(f\) is continuous.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 17

3.3 Homeomorphisms We now introduce the notion of topological equivalence, also known as homeomorphism.

nodes.title: Homeomorphisms
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. A bijective map \(f : X \to Y\) with the property that both \(f\) and \(f -1 : Y \to X\) are continuous, is called a homeo- morphism. If there exists a homeomorphism \(f : X \to Y , we\) say that \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomor- phic and write \(X \cong Y\) .

A homeomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) gives a one-to-one correspondence between open sets in \(X\) and \(Y\) . As a result, any property of a topological space that can be expressed in terms of its elements and its open subsets is preserved by homeomorphisms. Such a property is called a topological property.

nodes.title: Let r be the topological space of the real numbers with the
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. The map \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) given by

\[f (x) = 2x - 1\]

is a homeomorphism. Let \(g : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) be given by

\(g\)(\(y\)) = (\(y + 1)\) then, clearly, \(g\)(\(f\) (\(x\))) = \(x\) and \(f\) (\(g\)(\(y\))) = \(y\) for all real numbers \(x\) and \(y\). Thus \(f\) is a bijection and \(f -1 = g\). From calculus we know that \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous. Hence, \(f\) is a homeomorphism.

nodes.title: Homeomorphic finite topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X = {a, b}, and\) let \(\mathcal{T}1 = {\emptyset, {a}, X}\) and \(\mathcal{T}2 = {\emptyset, {b}, X}\) be two topolo- gies on \(X\). The map \(f : X \to X\) given by \(f\) (\(a\)) = \(b\) and \(f\) (\(b\)) = \(a\) is clearly a continuous bijection (with the domain given \(\mathcal{T}1\) as topology, and the codomain given \(\mathcal{T}2\) as topology). Also, \(f\) is its own inverse: \(f = f -1\) . Hence, \(f\) is a homeomorphism and (\(X, \mathcal{T}1\) ) ≅ (\(X, \mathcal{T}2\) ).

Homeomorphisms are continuous bijections, but the converse is not true.

nodes.title: Continuous bijection that is not a homeomorphism
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X = {a, b}\). The identity map id ∶ \(X \to X\) where the domain is given the discrete topology and the codomain is given the indiscrete topology is a continuous bijection but not a homeomorphism: the inverse map is not continuous.

The following theorem says that being homeomorphic is an equivalence relation on any set of topological spaces.

nodes.title: Homeomorphism is an equivalence relation
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X, Y\) and \(Z\) be topological spaces.

Reflexivity The identity map id ∶ \(X \to X\) (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology), given by id(\(x\)) = \(x\) for \(x \in X, is\) a homeomorphism.

Symmetry If \(f : X \to Y\) is a homeomorphism, then \(f -1 : Y \to X\) is also a homeomor- phism.

Transitivity If \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) are homeomorphisms, then \(g\)◦\(f : X \to Z\) is also

18 3.4. Closed sets

a homeomorphism.

Proof. The identity map id ∶ \(X \to X\) (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology) is clearly continuous and bijective. As the identity map is its own inverse, then it is also a homeomorphism. Hence, \(X \cong X\) and so ≅ satisfies the reflexivity condition for an equivalence relation. If \(f : X \to Y\) is a homeomorphism, then \(f -1 : Y \to X\) is also a homeomorphism: \(f -1\) is a continuous bijection with continuous inverse (\(f -1\) )−1 = \(f : X \to Y\) . Hence, \(X \cong Y\) if and only if \(Y \cong X\). Thus ≅ satisfies the symmetry condition for an equivalence relation.

nodes.title: Closed subsets
nodes.originalText:
A subset \(K\) of a topological space \(X\) is closed in \(X\) if and only if the complement \(K\) \(c = X\) ⧵ \(K\) is open in \(X\).

This is completely analogous to how we defined closed subsets in metric spaces, cf. Definition 2.9.

nodes.title: Let x be a discrete topological space since every subset of
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a discrete topological space. Since every subset of \(X\) is open in \(X, it\) follows that every subset of \(X\) is also closed in \(X\).

nodes.title: Let x be an indiscrete topological space the only subsets of
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be an indiscrete topological space. The only subsets of \(X\) that are closed in \(X\) are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) (which are also the only subsets that are open in \(X\)).

Recall that in a discrete topological space, all the singletons are open sets. This is usually not the case.

nodes.title: Let r be the topological space of the real numbers with the
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then every subset [\(a, b] = {x \in \mathbb{R}\) ∣ \(a \le x \le b} \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) is closed in \(\mathbb{R}: the\) complement [\(a, b]c = \mathbb{R}\) ⧵ [\(a, b] = (\)−∞, \(a\)) ∪ (\(b\), ∞) is a union of open sets in \(\mathbb{R}, and\) hence, is open in \(\mathbb{R}\). Furthermore, all the singletons are closed: the complement {\(a\)}\(c = \mathbb{R}\) ⧵ {\(a\)} = (−∞, \(a\)) ∪ (\(a\), ∞) is a union of open sets in \(\mathbb{R}, and\) hence, is open in \(\mathbb{R}\).

By passing to complements we get the following theorem.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 19

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space.

(1) Both \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) are closed (as subsets) in \(X\).

(2) The intersection of any subcollection of closed sets in \(X\) is closed in \(X\).

(3) The union of any finite subcollection of closed sets in \(X\) is closed in \(X\).

It follows that we could have defined a topological space \(X\) by specifying a collection of subsets of \(X\) satisfying the three statements in Theorem 3.28 where we would say that a subset of \(X\) is open in \(X\) if its complement is closed in \(X\). We end this section with a theorem describing the connection between continuous maps and closed sets. We will need the following definition.

nodes.title: Closure
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). The closure of \(A, written\) \(A, is\) the intersection of all subsets of \(X\) that contain \(A\) and which are closed in \(X\).

From the definition it follows that \(A\) is the smallest subset of \(X\) that contains \(A\) and which is closed in \(X\). Furthermore, if \(A\) is closed in \(X, then\) \(A = A\). There is an analogous definition for open sets where we take union instead of intersection. We can define the interior of \(A, written\) Int(\(A\)), to be the union of all subsets of \(X\) that are contained in \(A\) and which are open in \(X\). It follows that Int(\(A\)) is the largest subset of \(X\) that is contained in \(A\) and which is open in \(X\). Furthermore, Int(\(A\)) ⊆ \(A \subseteq A\).

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space consisting of the set a b c and
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a topological space consisting of the set {\(a, b, c}\) and the topology \(\mathcal{T} = {\emptyset, {a}, {a, b}, X}\). Then the closed subsets in \(X\) are \(\emptyset, {b, c}, {c}\) and \(X\). Thus the intersection of all of the closed subsets that contain {\(b\)} is simply {\(b, c}\)∩\(X = {b, c}, and\) hence, {\(b\)} = {\(b, c}\).

nodes.title: Let r be the topological space of the real numbers with the
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Assume that \(a < b\) are real numbers. Then (\(a, b] = [a, b]\) and Int((\(a, b]) = (a, b)\).

Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space. If we consider \(X\) as a topological space with the metric topology \(\mathcal{T}d , the\) closure \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) of an open ball \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) about \(x \in X\) is, in general, not the same as the closed ball \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)). If \(d\) is the discrete metric and \(X\) has at least two elements, then \(B\)(\(x\); 1) = {\(x\)} while \(B\)(\(x\); 1) = \(X\). It is always the case that \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)) ⊆ \(B\)(\(x\); \(r\)).

nodes.title: Dense
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). We say that \(A\) is dense in \(X\) if \(A = X\).

From the definition it follows that \(A\) is dense in \(X\) if and only if \(A\) ∩ \(U \ne \emptyset\) for every nonempty subset \(U\) of \(X\) which is open in \(X\).

nodes.title: Let r be the topological space of the real numbers with the
nodes.originalText: Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the subset ℚ of rational numbers is dense in \(\mathbb{R}\): ℚ = \(\mathbb{R}\).

nodes.title: For any topological space x the subset x is dense in x if x
nodes.originalText:
For any topological space \(X, the\) subset \(X\) is dense in \(X\). If \(X\) is a discrete topological space, then the subset \(X\) is the only dense subset in \(X\).

20 3.5. Exercises

nodes.title: Let f x y be a map between topological spaces then the follo
nodes.originalText:
Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a map between topological spaces. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) \(f\) is continuous;

(2) for every subset \(A\) of \(X, we\) have \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(f\) (\(A\));

(3) for every closed subset \(B\) of \(Y , the\) preimage \(f -1\) (\(B\)) of \(B\) under \(f\) is closed in \(X\).

Proof. By passing to complements, it follows readily that (1) and (3) are equivalent. We will prove that (2) is equivalent to (3). Assume (2). Let \(B\) be a subset of \(Y\) that is closed in \(Y , and\) let \(A = f -1\) (\(B\)). We must show that \(A\) is closed. We have \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(B\). If \(x \in A, then\) \(f\) (\(x\)) ∈ \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(B\). Hence, \(x \in f -1\) (\(B\)) = \(A\). In other words, \(A \subseteq A\). Thus \(A = A, and\) hence, \(f -1\) (\(B\)) is closed in \(X\). Now assume (3). Let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). We must show that \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(f\) (\(A\)). Since \(f\) (\(A\)) is closed in \(Y , it\) follows by assumption that \(f -1\) (\(f\) (\(A\))) is closed in \(X\). Furthermore, \(A \subseteq f -1\) (\(f\) (\(A\))) ⊆ \(f -1\) (\(f\) (\(A\))). Since \(f -1\) (\(f\) (\(A\))) is closed in \(X, it\) follows that \(A \subseteq f -1\) (\(f\) (\(A\))). Hence, \(f\) (\(A\)) ⊆ \(f\) (\(A\)).

3.5 Exercises

Exercise 3.1 Let \(X = {a, b, c, d}\). Show that \(\mathcal{T} = {\emptyset, {a}, {a, b}, {a, b, c}, {b, c, d}, X}\) is not a topology on \(X\). Find a topology \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ (different from the discrete topology) on \(X\) such that \(\mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{T}\) ′.

Exercise 3.2 Let \(X\) be a non-empty set, and let \(x0\) be an element of \(X\). Show that

\(\mathcal{T} = {U \subseteq X\) ∣ \(x0 \notin U\) or \(X\) ⧵ \(U\) is finite}

is a topology on \(X\).

Exercise 3.3 Let \(X\) be a set, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). Define the coarsest topology on \(X\) such that \(A\) is open in \(X\).

Exercise 3.4 Show that the discrete topology \(\mathcal{T}disc\) is finer than the cofinite topology \(\mathcal{T}cof\) on any set \(X\).

Exercise 3.5 Let \(X = {a, b, c, d}\). Find two topologies \(\mathcal{T}1\) and \(\mathcal{T}2\) with \(\mathcal{T}1 \ne \mathcal{T}2\) such that a bijection \(f : X \to X\) is a homeomorphism (where the domain is given \(\mathcal{T}1\) as topology and the codomain is given \(\mathcal{T}2\) as topology).

Exercise 3.6 Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) and \(B\) be subsets of \(X\).

\[(a) Assume that A \subseteq B. Show that A \subseteq B.\]

\[(b) Show that A \cup B = A \cup B.\]

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 21

We say that a set \(A\) intersects or meets a set \(B\) if \(A\) ∩ \(B \ne \emptyset\).

Exercise 3.7 Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). Show that \(x \in A\) if and only if every neighborhood of \(x\) intersects \(A\).

Exercise 3.8 Let \(X = {a, b, c, d, e}, and\) let

\[\mathcal{T} = {\emptyset, {a, b}, {a, b, c}, {a, b, d, e}, {b}, {b, c}, {b, d, e}, {b, c, d, e}, {d, e}, X}\]

be a topology on \(X\). Is the subset {\(a, b}\) dense in \(X\)?

4. Generating topologies

4.1 Generating topologies from subsets The following theorem tells us how we may extract a third topology from two other topologies on the same set.

nodes.title: The intersection of two topologies is a topology
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let \(\mathcal{T}1\) and \(\mathcal{T}2\) be two topologies on \(X\). Then \(\mathcal{T}1\) ∩ \(\mathcal{T}2\) is also a topology on \(X\).

Proof. Clearly, \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) are in \(\mathcal{T}1\) ∩ \(\mathcal{T}2 , so\) T1 is satisfied. Let {\(U\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be a collection of sets such that \(U\lambda \in \mathcal{T}1\) ∩ \(\mathcal{T}2\) for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ where Λ is some ⋃ index set. Then, for \(i = 1, 2, U\lambda \in \mathcal{T}i\) for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ. Thus \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(U\lambda \in \mathcal{T}i\) for \(i = 1, 2\). Hence, ⋃ \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(U\lambda \in \mathcal{T}1\) ∩ \(\mathcal{T}2 , and\) so, T2 is satisfied. Let {\(U1 , U2\) , … , \(Un\) } be a finite subcollection of \(\mathcal{T}\) . We need to prove that T3 is satisfied, i.e., \(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Un \in \mathcal{T}\) . Note that if \(U1 , U2\) , … , \(Un \in \mathcal{T}i\) then \(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Un \in \mathcal{T}i\) for \(i = 1, 2\). Hence, \(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Un \in \mathcal{T}1\) ∩ \(\mathcal{T}2\) . Thus T3 is satisfied.

Theorem ⋂ 4.1 may be extended to hold for a family of topologies: if {\(\mathcal{T}\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ is a family of topologies on \(X, then\) \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(\mathcal{T}\lambda\) is also a topology on \(X\). If we follow the convention that for subsets \(S\) of a fixed (large) set \(U\) , ⋂ \(S = U, S\in\emptyset\)

then the extended version of Theorem 4.1 may also include an empty family {\(\mathcal{T}\lambda\) }\(\lambda\in\emptyset\) of topologies with ⋂ \(\mathcal{T}\lambda = \mathcal{P}(X), \lambda\in\emptyset\)

i.e., the discrete topology on \(X\) (with our fixed (large) set \(U\) being equal to \(\mathcal{P}\)(\(X\))). However, not all mathemati- cians follow this convention. Thus we will in general not define the intersection of an empty family.

The union of two topologies is not necessarily a topology.

nodes.title: Let x a b c and let a a b x and c x be two
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X = {a, b, c}, and\) let \(\mathcal{T}1 = {\emptyset, {a}, {a, b}, X}\) and \(\mathcal{T}2 = {\emptyset, {c}, X}\) be two topologies on \(X\). Then \(\mathcal{T}1\) ∩ \(\mathcal{T}2 = {\emptyset, X}\) is the indiscrete topology on \(X\) while

\[\mathcal{T}1 \cup \mathcal{T}2 = {\emptyset, {a}, {a, b}, {c}, X}\]

is not a topology on \(X: \mathcal{T}1\) ∪ \(\mathcal{T}2\) does not satisfy T2.

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 23

Recall from Section 3.1 that for any set \(X\) the discrete topology \(\mathcal{T}disc\) is the largest topology we may equip \(X\) with, and the indiscrete topology \(\mathcal{T}ind\) is the smallest topology we may equip \(X\) with. For any topology \(\mathcal{T}\) on \(X\) we have \(\mathcal{T}ind \subseteq \mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{T}disc\) .

That is, we have partially ordered topologies on \(X\) by inclusion. Let \(X\) be a set. We often want to have a collection of subsets  of \(X to be\) the open subsets of a topology on \(X\).

nodes.title: Topology generated by a collection of subsets
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be a collection of subsets of \(X\). The topology generated by  is the topology ⋂ ⟨⟩ = \(\mathcal{T}\) \(\mathcal{T} topology\) ⊆\(\mathcal{T}\)

on \(X\).

In other words, ⟨⟩ contains  and for any other topology \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ containing , we have ⟨⟩ ⊆ \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ . Thus ⟨⟩ is unique.

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let then is the same as the indiscrete to
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a set, and let  = \(\emptyset\). Then ⟨⟩ is the same as the indiscrete topology on \(X, i\).e., ⟨⟩ = \(\mathcal{T}ind = {\emptyset, X}\).

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let be the collection of all the singleto
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be the collection of all the singletons of \(X, i\).e.,  = {{\(x\)} ∣ \(x \in X}\). Then ⟨⟩ is the same as the discrete topology on \(X, i\).e.,

\[⟨⟩ = \mathcal{T}disc = \mathcal{P}(X).\]

4.2 Basis for a topology It is often convenient to define a topology \(\mathcal{T}\) on a set \(X\) by only specifying a subcollection  of \(\mathcal{T}\) satisfying certain properties. The open subsets of \(X\) are then precisely the unions of subcollections of . In this way, we say the basis determines, or generates, the topology.

nodes.title: Basis
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set. A basis for a topology on \(X\) is a collection  of subsets of \(X\) such that

B1 for each \(x \in X, there\) is a \(B\) ∈  such that \(x \in B\);

B2 if \(B1 , B2\) ∈  and \(x \in B1\) ∩ \(B2 , then\) there is a \(B3\) ∈  such that \(x \in B3 \subseteq B1\) ∩ \(B2\) .

The elements of  are sometimes referred to as basis elements.

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let be the collection of all the singleto
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be the collection of all the singletons of \(X\). Then  is a basis for the discrete topology on \(X\).

24 4.2. Basis for a topology

nodes.title: Let x d be a metric space then the collection of open balls
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space. Then the collection of (open) \(\epsilon-balls\)

\[ = {B(x; \epsilon) \mid x \in X, \epsilon > 0}\]

is a basis for the metric topology \(\mathcal{T}d , as\) defined in Theorem 3.2, on \(X\).

The following theorem describes a topology generated by a basis.

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let be basis for a topology on x the coll
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be basis for a topology on \(X\). The collection \(\mathcal{T}\) generated by  of subsets \(U\) of \(X\) with the property that for each \(x \in U\) there is a basis element \(B\) ∈  with \(x \in B \subseteq U\) is a topology on \(X\).

Proof. Clearly, \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) are both in \(\mathcal{T}\) . Hence, T1 is satisfied. ⋃ Let {\(U\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be a subcollection of \(\mathcal{T}\) . Let \(V = \lambda\)∈Λ \(U\lambda\) . We need to prove that \(V \in \mathcal{T}\) . Let \(x \in V\) . Then there is a \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ such that \(x \in U\lambda\) . Since \(U\lambda \in \mathcal{T} , there\) is a basis element \(B\) ∈  such that \(x \in B \subseteq U\lambda\) . As \(U\lambda \subseteq V , it\) follows that \(x \in B \subseteq V\) . Hence, \(V \in \mathcal{T} , and\) so, T2 is satisfied. We need to prove that T3 is satisfied, that is, for a finite subcollection {\(U1 , U2\) , … , \(Un\) } of \(\mathcal{T} , we\) have \(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Un \in \mathcal{T}\) . We will prove the case for \(n = 2\). The general result then follows by an induction argument. Let \(x \in U1\) ∩ \(U2\) . Since \(U1\) ∩ \(U2 \subseteq Ui\) we have \(x \in Ui , and\) thus there is a basis element \(Bi\) ∈  with \(x \in Bi \subseteq Ui\) for \(i = 1, 2\). Hence, \(x \in B1\) ∩ \(B2 \subseteq U1\) ∩ \(U2\) . By B2 there is a basis element \(B3\) ∈  with \(x \in B3 \subseteq B1\) ∩ \(B2\) . Thus \(x \in B3 \subseteq U1\) ∩ \(U2 , and\) so, \(U1\) ∩ \(U2 \in \mathcal{T}\) .

The topology generated by a basis may also be described using the following theorem.

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let be a basis for a topology on x then i
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be a basis for a topology \(\mathcal{T}\) on \(X\). Then \(\mathcal{T}\) is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of .

Proof. Let \(B\) ∈  be any basis element. Then for each \(x \in B\) we obviously have \(x \in B\) and \(B \subseteq B\). Thus \(B \in \mathcal{T}\) . It follows that any union of basis elements is a union of elements of \(\mathcal{T} , and\) hence, is in \(\mathcal{T}\). Conversely, let \(U \in \mathcal{T}\) . For each \(x \in U\) there is a \(Bx\) ∈  with \(x \in Bx\) and \(Bx \subseteq U\) . Then ⋃ \(U = x\inU\) \(Bx , and\) thus, \(U\) is the union of elements of .

We end this section with a theorem describing a criterion for whether one topology is finer than another when both topologies are described using bases.

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let and be bases for topologies and
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let 1 and 2 be bases for topologies \(\mathcal{T}1\) and \(\mathcal{T}2 , respec- tively, on\) \(X\). Then the following are equivalent:

(1) \(\mathcal{T}2\) is finer than \(\mathcal{T}1 , i\).e., \(\mathcal{T}1 \subseteq \mathcal{T}2\) .

(2) For each \(B1\) ∈ 1 and each \(x \in B1\) there is a \(B2\) ∈ 2 such that \(x \in B2 \subseteq B1\) .

Proof. Assume (1). Let \(B1\) ∈ 1 and \(x \in B1\) . Since \(B1 \in \mathcal{T}1\) and \(\mathcal{T}1 \subseteq \mathcal{T}2\) we have \(B1 \in \mathcal{T}2\) . Furthermore, as \(\mathcal{T}2\) is the topology generated by 2 there is a \(B2\) ∈ 2 such that \(x \in B2\) where \(B2 \subseteq B1\) . Hence, (2) is satisfied.

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 25

Now assume (2). Let \(U \in \mathcal{T}1\) . We must prove that \(U \in \mathcal{T}2\) . Since 1 generates \(\mathcal{T}1 , then\) for each \(x \in U\) there is a \(B1\) ∈ 1 such that \(x \in B1 \subseteq U\) . By assumption there is a \(B2\) ∈ 2 such that \(x \in B2 \subseteq B1\) . Hence, \(B2 \subseteq U , and\) so, \(U \in \mathcal{T}2\) . Thus (1) is satisfied.

In order to have \(\mathcal{T}1 \subseteq \mathcal{T}2\) it is not necessary to have 1 ⊆ 2 , i.e., each basis element in 1 need not be a basis element in 2 . However, for each basis element \(B1\) ∈ 1 and each point \(x \in B1\) there should be some (possibly) smaller basis element \(B2\) ∈ 2 such that \(x \in B2 \subseteq B1\) .

4.3 Subbasis for a topology Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be a collection of subsets of \(X\). We can form a basis  for a topology by simply taking all finite intersections \(n\) ⋂ \(B= Si\) \(i=1\) of elements of . Thus the open sets in the topology generated by this basis are all unions of such basis elements \(B, cf\). Theorem 4.10. Hence, the open sets are all unions of all finite intersections of elements of . The collection  is then referred to as a subbasis. We will impose the restriction that the union of the elements of  is equal to \(X to avoid\) ambiguity about the intersection of an empty collection.

nodes.title: Subbasis
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a set. A subbasis for a topology on \(X\) is a collection  of subsets of \(X\) whose union equals \(X\).

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let be a subbasis for a topology on x the
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on \(X\). The collection  consisting of all finite intersections of elements of  is a basis for a topology on \(X\) and is called the basis associated to .

Proof. Each \(x \in X\) must lie in some \(S\) ∈ . Hence, \(x \in S\). Thus \(x\) is an element of the basis element \(S\) in , and so, B1 is satisfied. ⋂ ⋂\(n\) ′ Let \(B1 = m\) \(i=1\) \(Si\) and \(B2 = i=1\) \(Si\) be two basis elements of , and let \(x \in B1\) ∩ \(B2\) . We must prove that there is a basis element \(B3\) ∈  such that \(x \in B3 \subseteq B1\) ∩ \(B2\) . Let (\(m\) ) ( \(n\) ) ⋂ ⋂ ′ \(B3 = Si\) ∩ \(Si\) . \(i=1\) \(i=1\)

Then \(B3\) is also a finite intersection of elements of , and hence, \(B3\) ∈  with \(x \in B3\) . Thus B2 is satisfied.

By combining the previous lemma with Theorem 4.10, we get the following lemma.

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let be a subbasis for a topology on x the
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on \(X\). The collection \(\mathcal{T}\) generated by  consisting of all unions of all basis elements of the associated basis  is a topology on \(X\).

When referring to the topology \(\mathcal{T}\) generated by the subbasis  we mean the topology generated by the associated basis . We have  ⊆  ⊆ \(\mathcal{T}\) .

26 4.4. Exercises

The following theorem provides an explicit description of the topology generated by a collection of subsets  of a set \(X\).

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let be a subbasis for a topology on x the
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let  be a subbasis for a topology on \(X\). Then there is a unique topology ⟨⟩ generated by  which is coarser than any other topology containing , where { } ⋃⋂ \(n\lambda\) | | ⟨⟩ = \(S\lambda,i\) | \(S\lambda,i\) ∈  . | \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(i=1\) |

In other words, ⟨⟩ is the topology for which  is a subbasis. Proof. Since the discrete topology \(\mathcal{T}disc = \mathcal{P}(X), there\) is at least one topology on \(X\) that contains . We know from Theorem 4.1 that taking the intersection of all topologies that contain  is again a topology which clearly still contains . By construction, ⟨⟩ is then contained in any other topology containing . Thus ⟨⟩ is the unique topology with this property. Let { } ⋃⋂ \(n\lambda\) | | \(\mathcal{T}\) = \(S\lambda,i\) | \(S\lambda,i\) ∈  . | \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(i=1\) | Then \(\mathcal{T}\) ⊆ ⟨⟩. We need to prove that they are equal. To do this we will prove that \(\mathcal{T}\) is a topology on \(X\) that contains . Hence, by the first part ⟨⟩ = \(\mathcal{T}\) . Since  is a subbasis for a topology on \(X, by\) Lemma 4.14 we know that the topology generated by  is equal to the collection of all unions of basis elements of the associated  to . Hence, \(\mathcal{T}\) is a topology on \(X\). We end this section with a theorem about continuity and (sub)basis.

nodes.title: Let x and y be topological spaces and let resp be a basis re
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces, and let  (resp., ) be a basis (resp., subbasis) for the topology on \(Y\) . Then a map \(f : X \to Y\) is continuous if and only if for each \(B\) ∈  (resp. \(S\) ∈ ) the preimage \(f -1\) (\(B\)) (resp., \(f -1\) (\(S\))) is open in \(X\).

Proof. We prove the statement about basis. Assume that \(f\) is continuous. Since each basis element \(B\) ∈  is open in \(Y , then\) by continuity \(f -1\) (\(B\)) is open in \(X\). Assume that for each \(B\) ∈  the preimage \(f -1\) (\(B\)) is open in \(X\). Let \(\mathcal{T}Y\) be the topology on \(Y\) . ⋃ Since every \(V \in \mathcal{T}Y\) is a union \(V = \lambda\)∈Λ \(B\lambda\) of basis elements \(B\lambda\) ∈ , we have ⋃ \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) = \(f -1\) (\(B\lambda\) ). \(\lambda\)∈Λ

Thus if each \(f -1\) (\(B\lambda\) ) is open in \(X, so\) is \(f -1\) (\(V\) ).

4.4 Exercises

Exercise 4.1 Let \(X = {a, b, c, d, e}, and\) let

\[\mathcal{T} = {\emptyset, {a, b}, {a, b, d, e}, {b}, {b, d, e}, {b, c, d, e}, {c, d, e}, {d, e}, X}\]

be a topology on \(X\). Show that  = {{\(a, b}, {b, d, e}, {c, d, e}}\) is a subbasis for \(\mathcal{T}\) . Is  ′ = {{\(a, b}, {b, c, d, e}, {d, e}}\) a subbasis for \(\mathcal{T}\) ?

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 27

Exercise 4.2 Let  be the collection of all open intervals (\(a, b) = {x \in \mathbb{R}\) ∣ \(a < x < b}\) in \(\mathbb{R}\).

(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on \(\mathbb{R}\). The topology generated by  is called the standard topology on \(\mathbb{R}\) denoted by \(\mathcal{T}std\) .

(b) Show that \(\mathcal{T}std = \mathcal{T}d\) where \(\mathcal{T}d\) is the metric topology obtained from the metric \(d\)(\(x, y) = |x - y|\).

Exercise 4.3 Show that

\[ = {(a, \infty) \mid a \in \mathbb{R}} \cup {(-\infty, b) \mid b \in \mathbb{R}}\]

is a subbasis for the standard topology on \(\mathbb{R}\).

Exercise 4.4 Let ℚ deonte the set of rational numbers, and let \(\mathbb{R}\) denote the set of real numbers. Show that  = {(\(a, b)\) ∣ \(a < b, a, b\) ∈ ℚ} is a basis for the standard topology on \(\mathbb{R}\) where (\(a, b) = {x \in \mathbb{R}\) ∣ \(a < x < b}\).

Exercise 4.5 Let  be the collection of all half-open intervals of the form [\(a, b) = {x \in \mathbb{R}\) ∣ \(a \le x < b}\) in \(\mathbb{R}\).

(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on \(\mathbb{R}\). The topology generated by  is called the lower limit topology on \(\mathbb{R}\).

(b) Find the closure of the subset (0, 1) of \(\mathbb{R}\) given the lower limit topology.

Exercise 4.6 For each \(n \in \mathbb{Z}, let\) { {\(n\)} if \(n\) is odd, \(B\)(\(n\)) = {\(n - 1, n, n + 1}\) if \(n\) is even.

Show that the collection  = {\(B\)(\(n\)) ∣ \(n \in \mathbb{Z}}\) is a basis for a topology on \(\mathbb{Z}\). The topology gen- erated by  is known as the digital line topology on \(\mathbb{Z}\). See [1, pp. 44–46] for some applications of this topology.

Exercise 4.7 Let  be the collection of all subsets of the form \(Aa,b = {az + b\) ∣ \(z \in \mathbb{Z}}\) of \(\mathbb{Z}, where\) \(a, b \in \mathbb{Z}\) and \(a \ne 0\). (The set \(Aa,b\) is known as an arithmetic progression.)

(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on \(\mathbb{Z}\).

(b) Show that there are infinitely many primes by using the topology generated by . (This topology is known as the arithmetic progression topology on \(\mathbb{Z}\) and it was used originally by Furstenberg [3] to show that there are infinitely many primes.)

28 4.4. Exercises

Exercise 4.8 Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on \(X\). Show that a subset \(A\) of \(X\) is dense in \(X\) if and only if every non-empty basis element in  intersects \(A\). (Recall that a set \(U\) intersects a set \(V\) if \(U\) ∩ \(V \ne \emptyset\).)

5. Constructing topological spaces

5.1 Subspaces Let \(A\) be a subset of a topological space \(X\). There is a natural way to define a topology on \(A\) that is based on the topology on \(X\).

nodes.title: Subspace topology
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). The collection \(\mathcal{T}A = {A\) ∩ \(U\) ∣ \(U\) is open in \(X\)} of subsets of \(A\) is called the subspace topology on \(A\).

The subspace topology is indeed a topology.

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let a be a subset of x then
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). Then the collection \(\mathcal{T}A = {A\) ∩ \(U\) ∣ \(U\) is open in \(X\)} is a topology on \(A\).

Proof. Let \(\mathcal{T}\) denote the topology on \(X\). Since \(\emptyset, X \in \mathcal{T} , \emptyset = A\) ∩ \(\emptyset\) and \(A = A\) ∩ \(X, then, clearly, \emptyset, A \in \mathcal{T}A\) . Hence, T1 is satisfied. Let {\(V\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be a collection of subsets of \(A\) who are open in \(A, i\).e., \(V\lambda \in \mathcal{T}A\) . We must show that ⋃ \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(V\lambda \in \mathcal{T}A\) . For each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ there is a \(U\lambda \in \mathcal{T}\) such that \(V\lambda = A\) ∩ \(U\lambda\) . Thus ⋃ ⋃( ) ⋃ \(V\lambda = A\) ∩ \(U\lambda = A\) ∩ \(U\lambda\) . \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(\lambda\)∈Λ ⋃ ⋃ Since \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(U\lambda \in \mathcal{T} , it\) follows that \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(V\lambda \in \mathcal{T}A\) . Hence, T2 is satisfied. Let \(V1 , V2\) , … , \(Vn\) be subsets of \(A\) that are open in \(A, i\).e., \(Vi \in \mathcal{T}A\) for \(i = 1, 2\), … , \(n\). We must ⋂ show that \(ni=1\) \(Vi \in \mathcal{T}A\) . For each \(i \in {1, 2\), … , \(n\)} there is a \(Ui \in \mathcal{T}\) such that \(Vi = A\) ∩ \(Ui\) . Thus

\[n n n \bigcap \bigcap ( ) \bigcap Vi = A \cap Ui = A \cap Ui . i=1 i=1 i=1\]

⋂\(n\) ⋂\(n\) Since \(i=1\) \(Ui \in \mathcal{T} , it\) follows that \(i=1\) \(Vi \in \mathcal{T}A\) . Hence, T3 is satisfied.

Let \(X\) be a topological space (with topology \(\mathcal{T}\) ), and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). If \(V\) is a subset of \(A, there\) are two possible meanings to the statement “\(V\) is open.” We can either take \(V to be\) open in \(X, i\).e., \(V \in \mathcal{T} , or\) we can take \(V to be\) open in \(A, i\).e., \(V \in \mathcal{T}A\) . In general, these do not mean the same thing. We say that \(V\) is closed in \(A\) if \(V\) is closed in the subspace topology of \(A\). In other words, if \(V\) is closed in \(A, then\) \(A\) ⧵ \(V\) is open in \(A\).

30 5.1. Subspaces

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let a be a subspace of x th
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). Then a subset \(K\) of \(A\) is closed in \(A\) if and only if there is a closed subset \(L\) of \(X\) with \(K = A\) ∩ \(L\).

Proof. Assume that \(K\) is a closed subset of \(A\). Then \(V = A\) ⧵ \(K\) is open in \(A, i\).e., there is an open subset \(U\) of \(X\) with \(V = A\) ∩ \(U\) . Moreover, \(L = X\) ⧵ \(U\) is a closed subset of \(X\) and

\[A \cap L = A \cap (X \setminus U ) = A \setminus (A \cap U ) = A \setminus V = K.\]

See Figure 5.1.

\(X\)

\(A\) \(U\)

\(K\)

\(V\)

\[Figure 5.1: K = A \cap L\]

Now assume that \(L\) is a closed subset of \(X\) with \(K = A\) ∩ \(L\). Then \(U = X\) ⧵ \(L\) is an open subset of \(X, and\) so, \(V = A\) ∩ \(U\) is an open subset of \(A\). Furthermore,

\[A \setminus K = A \setminus (A \cap L) = A \cap (X \setminus L) = A \cap U = V .\]

See Figure 5.2. Hence, \(K\) is a closed subset of \(A\).

\(X\) \(L\) \(A\)

\(V\)

\(K\)

\[Figure 5.2: V = A \setminus K\]

The next example illustrates the fact that we may have subsets of a topological space \(X\) that are open in the subspace \(A\) but which are not open in \(X\).

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 31

nodes.title: Let r denote the set of real numbers equipped with the stand
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2, and let \(I = [0, 1]\) be a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}\). Then sets of the form [0, \(a\)) and (\(a, 1]\) with 0 < \(a < 1\) are open in \(I\) but not in \(\mathbb{R}\).

The following theorem describes how we may extract a basis for the subspace topology on \(A\) from the basis of a topology on \(X\).

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let be a basis for the topo
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on \(X\). If \(A\) is a subset of \(X, then\) the collection

\[A = {A \cap B \mid B \in }\]

is a basis for the subspace topology on \(A\).

Proof. We need to prove that \(A\) is a basis for the subspace topology on \(A\). We first prove that \(A\) is a basis for a topology on \(A, and\) then that the topology generated by \(A\) equals the subspace topology on \(A\). First note that each \(B\) ∈  is open in \(X, and\) so, each \(A\) ∩ \(B\) ∈ \(A\) is open in \(A\). For each \(x \in X\) there is a basis element \(B\) ∈  such that \(x \in B\). Let \(x \in A\). Since \(A = A\) ∩ \(X, there\) must be a basis element \(B\) ∈  such that \(x \in A\) ∩ \(B\). Hence, B1 holds. Now let \(x \in A\) ∩ (\(B1\) ∩ \(B2\) ) where \(B1\) and \(B2\) are basis elements of . Since  is a basis for the topology on \(X, it\) follows that there is a basis element \(B3\) of  such that \(B3 \subseteq B1\) ∩ \(B2\) and \(x \in A\) ∩ \(B3 \subseteq A\) ∩ (\(B1\) ∩ \(B2\) ). Hence, B2 is satisfied. Thus \(A\) is a basis for a topology on \(A\). Let \(\mathcal{T}A\) be the subspace topology on \(A\). We want to prove that the topology \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ generated by \(A\) is equal to \(\mathcal{T}A\) . If \(A\) ∩ \(U \in \mathcal{T}A\) and \(x \in A\) ∩ \(U , then, using\) the fact that  is a basis for the topology on \(X, we\) have \(B\) ∈  such that \(x \in A\) ∩ \(B \subseteq A\) ∩ \(U\) . Thus \(A\) ∩ \(U \in \mathcal{T}\) ′ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10 we know that \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of \(A\) . Hence, if \(W \in \mathcal{T}\) ′ then \(W\) equals a union of elements of \(A\) . Since each element of \(A\) belongs to \(\mathcal{T}A\) and \(\mathcal{T}A\) is a topology, \(W\) also belongs to \(\mathcal{T}A\) .

We end this section with an alternative description of the subspace topology. Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(T\) be a set. There do exist topologies on \(T\) that make \(f : T \to X\) continuous, e.g., the discrete topology. If \(\mathcal{T}f\) is the intersection of all topologies on \(T\) such that \(f\) is continuous, then \(\mathcal{T}f\) is the coarsest topology for which \(f\) is continuous and

\(\mathcal{T}f = {f -1\) (\(U\) ) ∣ \(U\) is open in \(X\)}.

From this we may define the subspace topology as follows: Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be subset of \(X\). The subspace topology on \(A\) is then the coarsest topology on \(A\) for which the inclusion \(i : A \to X, given\) by \(i\)(\(x\)) = \(x\) for \(x \in A, is\) continuous. This coincides with our previous definition as \(i-1\) (\(U\) ) = \(A\) ∩ \(U\) for any subset \(U\) of \(X\). Thus

\(\mathcal{T}i = {i-1\) (\(U\) ) ∣ \(U\) is open in \(X\)} = {\(A\) ∩ \(U\) ∣ \(U\) is open in \(X\)},

and hence, \(\mathcal{T}i = \mathcal{T}A\) . The following theorem describes a universal property for the subspace topology.

32 5.1. Subspaces

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let a be a subset of x then
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). Then the subspace topology on \(A\) is the only topology on \(A\) with the following universal property: for every topo- logical space \(Y\) and every map \(f : Y \to A, f\) is continuous if and only if \(i\)◦\(f : Y \to X\) is continuous where \(i : A \to X\) is the inclusion map given by \(i\)(\(x\)) = \(x\) for \(x \in A\).

\(X\) \(i\)◦\(f\) \(i\)

\(Y\) \(A\) \(f\)

Proof. We will first prove that the subspace topology \(\mathcal{T}A\) has the universal property that for every topological space \(Y\) and every map \(f : Y \to A, f\) is continuous if and only if \(i\)◦\(f : Y \to X\) is continuous. Then we will prove that \(\mathcal{T}A\) is the only topology on \(A\) with this property. Consider \(A\) as a subspace of \(X\). Assume that \(f\) is continuous. Since the inclusion map \(i\) is con- tinuous (with \(A\) given the subspace topology), and the composition of two continuous maps is again continuous, cf. Theorem 3.16, it follows that \(i\)◦\(f\) is continuous. Now assume that \(i\)◦\(f\) is continuous. Let \(V\) be an open set in \(A, i\).e., \(V = A\) ∩ \(U\) for some open set \(U\) in \(X\). Since

\[f -1 (V ) = f -1 (A \cap U ) = f -1 (i-1 (U )) = (i◦f )-1 (U )\]

is open in \(Y\) by continuity of \(i\)◦\(f , it\) follows that \(f\) is continuous. Thus the subspace topology \(\mathcal{T}A\) has the desired property. Let \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ be a topology on \(A\) with the universal property that for every topological space \(Y\) and every map \(f : Y \to A, f\) is continuous if and only if \(i\)◦\(f : Y \to X\) is continuous. We must show that \(\mathcal{T}A = \mathcal{T}\) ′ . Let \(\mathcal{T}\) be the topology on \(X, and\) let \(A\) be given the topology \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ . First let \(Y = A\) with the subspace topology. Then for \(f = id : (A, \mathcal{T}A\) ) → (\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ), we have \(i\)◦ id = \(i : (A, \mathcal{T}A\) ) → (\(X, \mathcal{T}\) ) which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

(\(X, \mathcal{T}\) ) \(i\)◦ id \(i\)

(\(A, \mathcal{T}A\) ) (\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) id

Thus any \(V \in \mathcal{T}\) ′ must also be an element of \(\mathcal{T}A , and\) so, \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ ⊆ \(\mathcal{T}A\) . Secondly let \(Y = A\) with \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ as its topology. Then, clearly, \(f = id : (A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) → (\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) is continu- ous. Thus by the universal property it follows that \(i\)◦ id = \(i : (A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) → (\(X, \mathcal{T}\) ) is continuous.

(\(X, \mathcal{T}\) ) \(i\)◦ id \(i\)

(\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) (\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) id

Thus for any \(U \in \mathcal{T} , we\) have \(i-1\) (\(U\) ) = \(A\) ∩ \(U \in \mathcal{T}\) ′ . That is, \(\mathcal{T}A \subseteq \mathcal{T}\) ′ . Hence, \(\mathcal{T}A = \mathcal{T}\) ′ .

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 33

5.2 Products Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. If we want to give the product \(X \times Y\) a topology, a first approach might be to take the collection  = {\(U \times V\) ∣ \(U\) is open in \(X\) and \(V\) is open in \(Y\) } as the topology on \(X \times Y\) . However,  is not a topology. The union of two elements of  is not necessarily of the form \(U \times V\) for some \(U\) open in \(X\) and some \(V\) open in \(Y\) . See Figure 5.3.

\(Y\)

\[V1 U1 \times V1\]

\[V2 U2 \times V2\]

\(X\) \(U1\) \(U2\)

Figure 5.3: The collection  of all products of open sets in \(X\) and in \(Y\) is not a topology on \(X \times Y\) .

We can remedy the situation by taking  as a basis instead. The topology generated from this basis is what we will take to be the product topology on \(X \times Y\) .

nodes.title: Product topology
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. The product topology on \(X \times Y\) is the topology generated by the basis

 = {\(U \times V\) ∣ \(U\) is open in \(X\) and \(V\) is open in \(Y\) }.

nodes.title: Let x and y be topological spaces then the collection
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. Then the collection

 = {\(U \times V\) ∣ \(U\) is open in \(X\) and \(V\) is open in \(Y\) }

is a basis for a topology on \(X \times Y\) .

Proof. Let (\(x, y) \in X \times Y\) . We need to show that there is a basis element \(U \times V\) ∈  such that (\(x, y) \in U \times V \subseteq X \times Y\) . Since \(X\) is open in \(X\) and \(Y\) is open in \(Y , we\) simply take \(U = X\) and \(V = Y\) . Thus B1 is satisfied. Now let (\(x, y) \in (U1 \times V1\) ) ∩ (\(U2 \times V2\) ) where \(U1 \times V1 , U2 \times V2\) ∈ . Since (\(U1 \times V1\) ) ∩ (\(U2 \times V2\) ) = (\(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ) × (\(V1\) ∩ \(V2\) ), and \(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) and \(V1\) ∩ \(V2\) are open in \(X\) and \(Y , respectively, it\) follows by letting \(U3 = U1\) ∩ \(U2\) and \(V3 = V1\) ∩\(V2\) that there is a basis element \(U3 \timesV3\) ∈  such that (\(x, y) \in U3 \timesV3 \subseteq (U1 \timesV1\) )∩(\(U2 \timesV2\) ). Hence, B2 holds. Thus  is a basis for a topology on \(X \times Y\) .

34 5.2. Products

The basis described in Lemma 5.8 is relatively large; it consists of pairs of every open set \(U\) in \(X\) and every open set \(V\) in \(Y\) . The following theorem describes a smaller basis for the product topology based on bases rather than whole topologies.

nodes.title: Let x and y be topological spaces if x is a basis for the to
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. If \(X\) is a basis for the topology on \(X\) and \(Y\) is a basis for the topology on \(Y , then\) the collection

\(X\timesY = {BX \times BY\) ∣ \(BX\) ∈ \(X\) and \(BY\) ∈ \(Y\) }

is a basis for the product topology on \(X \times Y\) .

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proof of Theorem 5.5 and adapt them to our current setting. First note that each \(BX \times BY\) ∈ \(X\) × \(Y\) is open in \(X \times Y\) as each \(BX\) is open in \(X\) and each \(BY\) is open in \(Y\) . Let (\(x, y) \in X \times Y\) . Using the fact that \(X\) and \(Y\) are bases for topologies on \(X\) and \(Y , respectively, there\) are basis elements \(BX\) ∈ \(X\) and \(BY\) ∈ \(Y\) such that (\(x, y) \in BX \times BY \subseteq X \times Y\) . Thus B1 is satisfied. Now let (\(x, y) \in (BX,1 \times BY ,1\) ) ∩ (\(BX,2 \times BY ,2\) ) where \(BX,1 , BX,2\) ∈ \(X\) and \(BY ,1 , BY ,2\) ∈ \(Y\) . Since (\(BX,1 \times BY ,1\) ) ∩ (\(BX,2 \times BY ,2\) ) = (\(BX,1\) ∩ \(BX,2\) ) × (\(BY ,1\) ∩ \(BY ,2\) ) and \(X\) and \(Y\) are bases for the topologies on \(X\) and \(Y , respectively, it\) follows that there are basis elements \(BX,3\) ∈ \(X\) and \(BY ,3\) ∈ \(Y\) such that \(x \in BX,3 \subseteq BX,1\) ∩ \(BX,2 , y \in BY ,3 \subseteq BY ,1\) ∩ \(BY ,2\) and (\(x, y) \in BX,3 \times BY ,3 \subseteq (BX,1\) ∩ \(BX,2\) ) × (\(BY ,1\) ∩ \(BY ,2\) ). Thus B2 holds. Hence, \(X\timesY\) is a basis for a topology on \(X \times Y\) . Let \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY\) be the product topology on \(X \times Y\) . We want to prove that the topology \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ generated by \(X\timesY\) is equal to \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY\) . Let \(W \in \mathcal{T}X\timesY , and\) let (\(x, y) \in W\) . Then there is an open set \(U\) in \(X\) and an open set \(V\) in \(Y\) such that (\(x, y) \in U \times V \subseteq W , cf\). Theorem 4.9. Since \(U\) is open in \(X\) and \(X\) is a basis for the topology on \(X, it\) follows that there is a basis element \(BX\) ∈ \(X\) such that \(x \in Bx \subseteq U\) . Likewise, there is a basis element \(BY\) ∈ \(Y\) such that \(y \in BY \subseteq V\) . Thus (\(x, y) \in BX \times BY \subseteq W , and\) so, \(W \in \mathcal{T}\) ′ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10, we know that \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of \(X\timesY\) . Hence, if \(W \in \mathcal{T}\) ′ then \(W\) equals a union of elements of \(X\timesY\) . Since each element of \(X\timesY\) belongs to \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY\) and \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY\) is a topology, \(W\) also belongs to \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY\) .
