Then the collection { \(X\timesY = {a, b} \times {1}, {b} \times {1}, {b, c, d, e} \times {1}, {a, b} \times {1, 2}, {b} \times {1, 2}, {b, c, d, e} \times {1, 2}, }\) {\(a, b} \times Y , {b} \times Y , {b, c, d, e} \times Y\)

is a basis for the product topology on \(X \times Y\) .

nodes.title: Let r denote the set of real numbers equipped with the stand
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf. Exercise 4.2. Then the collection \(\mathbb{R}2 = {(a, b) \times (c, d)\) ∣ \(a < b, c < d}\) of open rectangular regions in \(\mathbb{R}2\) is a basis for the product topology on \(\mathbb{R}2 = \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}, as\) a basis for the standard topology of \(\mathbb{R}\) is the collection of open intervals of the form (\(a, b)\) where \(a < b\).

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 35

nodes.title: Let s denote the circle x y r2 x2 y2 seen as a subspace
nodes.originalText:
Let \(S\) 1 denote the circle {(\(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}2\) ∣ \(x2 + y2 = 1}\) seen as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}2\) (with the standard topology), i.e., \(S\) 1 is given the subspace topology. A basis element \(B\) for the product topology on the torus \(T\) 2 = \(S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 is illustrated in Figure 5.4. Note that the surface depicted in Figure 5.4 is homeomorphic to \(T\) 2 ; it is the surface of revolution generated by revolving a circle, say of radius 1 in the \(xz-plane\) with center (2, 0, 0), about an axis, e.g., the \(z-axis\).

Figure 5.4: An illustration of a basis element \(B\) for the product topology on \(T\) 2 = \(S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 .

We end this section with an alternative description of the product topology. The map pr 1 ∶ \(X\timesY \to X\) given by pr 1 (\(x, y) = x\) for (\(x, y) \in X \times Y\) is called the projection of \(X \times Y\) onto \(X\). Similarly, the map pr 2 ∶ \(X \times Y \to Y\) given by pr 2 (\(x, y) = y\) for (\(x, y) \in X \times Y\) is called the projection of \(X \times Y\) onto \(Y\) .

nodes.title: Let x and y be topological spaces let pr xy x and pr xy
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. Let pr 1 ∶ \(X\timesY \to X\) and pr 2 ∶ \(X\timesY \to Y\) be the projections of \(X \times Y\) onto its first and second factors, respectively. The product topology is the only topology on \(X \times Y\) with the following universal property: for every topological space \(Z\) and every map \(f : Z \to X \times Y , f\) is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦\(f : Z \to X\) and pr 2 ◦\(f : Z \to Y\) are continuous.

\[X\timesY X\timesY f f pr 1 pr 2\]

\(Z\) \(X\) \(Z\) \(Y\) pr 1 ◦\(f\) pr 2 ◦\(f\)

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proof of Theorem 5.6 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the product topology \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY\) has the universal property that for every topolog- ical space \(Z\) and every map \(f : Z \to X \times Y , f\) is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦\(f : Z \to X\) and pr 2 ◦\(f : Z \to Y\) are continuous. Let \(X \times Y\) be given the product topology, and let \(f : Z \to X \times Y\) be continuous. Since pr −1 1 (\(U\) ) = \(U \times Y\) for an open set \(U\) in \(X\) and \(Y\) is open in \(Y , it\) follows that pr 1 is continuous. Likewise, pr 2 ∶ \(X\) ×

36 5.2. Products

\(Y \to Y\) is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 both pr 1 ◦\(f\) and pr 2 ◦\(f\) are continuous. Now assume that pr 1 ◦\(f\) and pr 2 ◦\(f\) are continuous. Let \(U \times V\) be a subset of \(X \times Y\) where \(U\) is an open set in \(X\) and \(V\) is an open set in \(Y\) . Since \(U \times V = pr -1\) (\(U\) ) ∩ pr −1 (\(V\) ) and \(f -1\) (\(U \times V\) ) = \(f -1\) (pr −1 (\(U\) ) ∩ pr −1 (\(V\) )) = \(f -1\) (pr −1 (\(U\) )) ∩ \(f -1\) (pr −1 (\(V\) )) = (pr 1 ◦\(f\) )−1 (\(U\) ) ∩ (pr 2 ◦\(f\) )−1 (\(V\) ) is open in \(Z\) by continuity of pr 1 ◦\(f\) and pr 2 ◦\(f , and\) subsets of the form \(U \times V\) form a basis for the product topology on \(X \times Y , cf\). Lemma 5.8, it follows that \(f\) is continuous. Let \(\mathcal{T}\) be a topology on \(X \times Y\) with the universal property that for every topological space \(Z\) and every map \(f : Z \to X \times Y , f\) is continuous if and only if pr 1 ◦\(f : Z \to X\) and pr 2 ◦\(f : Z \to Y\) are continuous. We must show that \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY = \mathcal{T}\) . Let \(\mathcal{T}X\) be the topology on \(X\) and \(\mathcal{T}Y\) be the topology on \(Y , and\) let \(X \times Y\) be given the topology \(\mathcal{T}\) . First let \(Z = X \times Y\) with the product topology. Then for \(f = id : (X \times Y , \mathcal{T}X\timesY\) ) → (\(X \times Y , \mathcal{T}\) ), we have pr 1 ◦ id = pr 1 ∶ (\(X \times Y , \mathcal{T}X\timesY\) ) → (\(X, \mathcal{T}X\) ) and pr 2 ◦ id = pr 2 ∶ (\(X \times Y , \mathcal{T}X\timesY\) ) → (\(Y , \mathcal{T}Y\) ) which are both continuous. Thus by the universal property id is continuous.

\[(X \times Y , \mathcal{T} ) (X \times Y , \mathcal{T} ) id id pr 1 pr 2\]

\[(X \times Y , \mathcal{T}X\timesY ) (X, \mathcal{T}X ) (X \times Y , \mathcal{T}X\timesY ) (Y , \mathcal{T}Y ) pr 1 ◦ id pr 2 ◦ id\]

Hence, any \(W \in \mathcal{T}\) must also be an element of \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY , and\) so, \(\mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{T}X\timesY\) . Secondly let \(Z = X \timesY\) with \(\mathcal{T}\) as its topology. Then, clearly, \(f = id : (X \timesY , \mathcal{T}\) ) → (\(X \timesY , \mathcal{T}\) ) is continuous. Thus by the universal property it follows that both pr 1 ◦ id = pr 1 ∶ (\(X \times Y , \mathcal{T}\) ) → (\(X, \mathcal{T}X\) ) and pr 2 ◦ id = pr 2 ∶ (\(X \times Y , \mathcal{T}\) ) → (\(Y , \mathcal{T}Y\) ) are continuous.

\[(X \times Y , \mathcal{T} ) (X \times Y , \mathcal{T} ) id id pr 1 pr 2\]

\[(X \times Y , \mathcal{T} ) (X, \mathcal{T}X ) (X \times Y , \mathcal{T} ) (Y , \mathcal{T}Y ) pr 1 ◦ id pr 2 ◦ id\]

Thus for each \(U \in \mathcal{T}X\) and \(V \in \mathcal{T}Y , we\) have pr −1 (\(U\) ) = \(U \times Y \in \mathcal{T}\) and pr −1 (\(V\) ) = \(X \times V \in \mathcal{T} , and\) so, \(U \times V = pr -1\) (\(U\) ) ∩ pr −1 (\(V\) ) ∈ \(\mathcal{T}\) . Hence, \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY \subseteq \mathcal{T}\) . Thus \(\mathcal{T}X\timesY = \mathcal{T}\) .

We can extend our discussion of the product topology from \(X \times Y to X1 \times X2\) × ⋯ × \(Xn\) where each \(Xi\) ∏ is a topological space. If we are to extend to the product \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(X\lambda , which\) we can think of as the set of maps ⋃ \(f\) ∶ Λ → \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(X\lambda\) where \(f\) (\(\lambda\)) ∈ \(X\lambda\) for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ, of an indexed family {\(X\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ of topological spaces we ∏ ∏ may proceed in two ways. We may equip \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(X\lambda\) with the topology generated by the basis \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(U\lambda\) where ∏ \(U\lambda\) is open in \(X\lambda\) for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ. This is known as the box topology. We may also equip \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(X\lambda\) with the ⋃ topology generated by the subbasis  = 𝜇∈Λ {pr −1 𝜇 (\(U\)𝜇 ) ∣ \(U\)𝜇 is open in \(X\)𝜇 }. This is known as the product ∏\(n topology\). For finite products \(i=1\) \(Xi\) the two topologies are the same. Also, the box topology is, in general, finer than the product topology. Finally, several results regarding finite products may be extended to arbitrary products when using the product topology but not the box topology.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 37

5.3 Quotient spaces Let \(X\) be a topological space. In Section 5.1, we discussed how to define the coarsest possible topology on a subset \(A\) of \(X\) such that the inclusion map \(i : A \to X\) is continuous. This is known as the subspace topology. If we let \(A\) be a set which is not necessarily a subset of \(X\) and we consider a surjective map 𝜋 ∶ \(X \to A, the\) quotient topology is the finest topology on \(A\) such that 𝜋 is continuous. The torus \(T\) 2 = \(S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 (see Figure 5.4) can be constructed by taking a rectangle and “gluing” its edges together in an appropriate way as shown in Figure 5.5. Such a construction involves the concept of quotient topology.

nodes.title: Equivalence classes
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a set, and let ∼ be an equivalence relation on \(X\). The equivalence class of \(x \in X\) is the subset

\[[x] = {y \in X \mid x ∼ y}\]

of \(X\). Let \(X\)∕∼ = {[\(x\)] ∣ \(x \in X}\) be the set of equivalence classes.

By definition, \(x \in [x]\) for each \(x \in X\) and [\(x\)] = [\(y\)] if and only if \(x\) ∼ \(y\). Moreover, two equivalence classes [\(x1\) ] and [\(x2\) ] are either disjoint or equal. Finally, the union of all equivalence classes equal \(X\).

nodes.title: Let x and a be sets and let x a be a surjective map then the
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(A\) be sets, and let 𝜋 ∶ \(X \to A\) be a surjective map. Then the map

\[𝜑 : X∕∼ \to A\]

given by 𝜑([\(x\)]) = 𝜋(\(x\)), where \(x1\) ∼ \(x2\) if and only if 𝜋(\(x1\) ) = 𝜋(\(x2\) ), is a bijection.

Proof. The map is well-defined since [\(x1\) ] = [\(x2\) ] only if \(x1\) ∼ \(x2 , and\) so, 𝜋(\(x1\) ) = 𝜋(\(x2\) ) by definition of the equivalence relation. It is injective since 𝜑([\(x1\) ]) = 𝜑([\(x2\) ]) implies 𝜋(\(x1\) ) = 𝜋(\(x2\) ), and so, \(x1\) ∼ \(x2 , i\).e., [\(x1\) ] = [\(x2\) ]. Finally, it is surjective since 𝜋 is; every element of \(A\) is of the form 𝜋(\(x\)) = 𝜑([\(x\)]) for some \(x \in X\).

Thus by Lemma 5.15 we can, up to a bijection, go back and forth between equivalence relations on \(X\) and surjective maps \(X \to A\).

38 5.3. Quotient spaces

nodes.title: Quotient space
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(A\) be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ \(X \to A\) be a surjective map. The quotient topology on \(A\) induced by 𝜋 is the collection of subsets \(U\) of \(A\) such that 𝜋 −1 (\(U\) ) is open in \(X\). We say that 𝜋 is a quotient map if \(A\) is given the quotient topology, and we call \(A\) the quotient space.

In other words, 𝜋 ∶ \(X \to A\) is quotient map if it is surjective and a subset \(U\) of \(A\) is open in \(A\) if and only if 𝜋 −1 (\(U\) ) is open in \(X\). Equivalently, 𝜋 is a quotient map if it is surjective and \(U\) is closed in \(A\) if and only if 𝜋 −1 (\(U\) ) is closed in \(X\). Clearly, a quotient map is continuous.

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space let a be a set and let x a be a
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(A\) be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ \(X \to A\) be a surjective map. Then the quotient topology on \(A\) induced by 𝜋 is a topology and it is the finest topology on \(A\) such that 𝜋 is continuous.

Proof. Since 𝜋 −1 (\(\emptyset\)) = \(\emptyset\) and 𝜋 −1 (\(A\)) = \(X, and\) both \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) are open in \(X, it\) follows that \(\emptyset\) and \(A\) are open in \(A\). Thus T1 holds. Let {\(U\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be a collection of subsets of \(A\) that are open in \(A\). Then 𝜋 −1 (\(U\lambda\) ) is open in \(X\) for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ. Since ( ) ⋃ ⋃ −1 𝜋 \(U\lambda\) = 𝜋 −1 (\(U\lambda\) ) \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(\lambda\)∈Λ ⋃ is a union of open sets in \(X, it\) must be open in \(X\). Hence, \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(U\lambda\) is open in \(A\). Thus T2 is satisfied. We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of the quotient topology on \(A\) induced by 𝜋 is also an element of the quotient topology on \(A\) induced by 𝜋. Let \(U1 , U2\) , … , \(Un\) be subsets of \(A\) that are open in \(A\). Then 𝜋 −1 (\(Ui\) ) is open in \(X\) for \(i = 1, 2\), … , \(n\). Since ( \(n\) ) \(n\) ⋂ ⋂ 𝜋 −1 \(Ui\) = 𝜋 −1 (\(Ui\) ) \(i=1\) \(i=1\)

is a (finite) intersection of open sets in \(X, it\) must be open in \(X\). Hence, \(U1\) ∩ \(U2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Un\) is open in \(A, and\) so, T3 is satisfied. Thus the quotient topology is a topology. Let \(\mathcal{T}\) be a topology on \(A\) such that 𝜋 is continuous. We must show that \(\mathcal{T}\) is coarser than the quotient topology. Since 𝜋 is continuous when \(A\) is given \(\mathcal{T}\) as its topology, we have for each \(V \in \mathcal{T}\) that 𝜋 −1 (\(V\) ) is open in \(X, and\) so, \(V\) is in the quotient topology. Hence, \(\mathcal{T}\) is coarser than the quotient topology.

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, let \(A = {a, b, c}, and\) let 𝜋∶ \(\mathbb{R} \to A\) be the map given by ⎧\(a\) \(x = 0\), ⎪ 𝜋(\(x\)) = ⎨\(b\) \(x < 0\), ⎪\(c\) \(x > 0\). ⎩ Then the quotient topology on \(A\) induced by 𝜋 is the collection {\(\emptyset, {b}, {b, c}, {c}, A}\) of subsets of \(A\).

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 39

nodes.title: Open and closed maps
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces, and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a continuous map. We say that \(f\) is an open map if for each subset \(U\) of \(X\) that is open in \(X\) the image \(f\) (\(U\) ) is open in \(Y\) . Likewise, we say that \(f\) is a closed map if for each subset \(V\) of \(X\) that is closed in \(X\) the image \(f\) (\(V\) ) is closed in \(Y\) .

If \(f : X \to Y\) is an open (closed) map and \(A\) is a subset of \(X\) then it is not true in general that \(f\) |\(A : A \to f\) (\(A\)) is an open (closed) map. However, if we also assume that \(A\) is an open (closed) subset of \(X, then\) \(f\) |\(A : A \to f\) (\(A\)) is an open (closed) map. Consider the case where \(A\) is open in \(X\) and \(f\) is an open map. Let \(U\) be a subset of \(A\) that is open in \(A, i\).e., \(U = A\) ∩ \(V\) for some open subset \(V\) of \(X\). Then \(U\) is also open in \(X, and\) hence, \(f\) |\(A\) (\(U\) ) = \(f\) (\(U\) ) is an open subset of \(Y\) . Furthermore, since \(f\) (\(A\)) is open in \(Y\) and \(f\) (\(U\) ) ⊆ \(f\) (\(A\)), \(f\) (\(U\) ) must be open in \(f\) (\(A\)).

nodes.title: Any homeomorphism is both open and closed however the conver
nodes.originalText: Any homeomorphism is both open and closed. However, the converse is, in general, not true. Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let ∗ be the space consisting of a single element. Then \(\mathbb{R}\) →∗ is both an open and a closed map but it is clearly not a homeomorphism.

nodes.title: Let x and y be two topological spaces and let f x y be a bij
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two topological spaces, and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a bijective continuous map. Then the following are equivalent:

(1) \(f\) is a homeomorphism;

(2) \(f\) is an open map;

(3) \(f\) is a closed map.

Proof. We will show that (1) is equivalent to (2) and that (2) is equivalent to (3). Since \(f\) is a bijection, we have ( −1 )−1 \(f\) (\(U\) ) = \(f\) (\(U\) )

for any subset \(U\) of \(X\). In particular, if \(U\) is an open subset of \(X\) and if we assume that \(f\) is a homeo- morphism then \(f\) (\(U\) ) must be open in \(Y\) as \(f -1\) is continuous. Hence, \(f\) is an open map. Furthermore, if we assume that \(f\) is open instead of being a homeomorphism then \(f\) (\(U\) ) is open in \(Y\) for \(U\) an open subset of \(X\). Hence, \(f -1 : Y \to X\) is continuous. Thus \(f\) is a homeomorphism. Hence, (1) is equivalent to (2). Assume that \(f\) is an open map. Since \(f\) is a bijection, we have

\[f (X \setminus U ) = f (U c ) = f (U )c = Y \setminus f (U ).\]

Thus \(f\) is closed: \(X\) ⧵ \(U\) is closed and \(Y\) ⧵ \(f\) (\(U\) ) is closed. The opposite implication follows by a completely analogous argument. Hence, (2) is equivalent to (3). This completes the proof.

The following lemma establishes a relation between open and closed maps, and quotient maps.

40 5.3. Quotient spaces

nodes.title: Let x and y be topological spaces and let x y be a surjectiv
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces, and let 𝜋 ∶ \(X \to Y\) be a surjective contin- uous map.

(1) If 𝜋 is in addition open then it is a quotient map.

(2) If 𝜋 is in addition closed then it is a quotient map.

Proof. Assume that 𝜋 is in addition open. Let \(V\) be a subset of \(Y\) . If \(V\) is open in \(Y\) then 𝜋 −1 (\(V\) ) is open in \(X\) by assumption of continuity of 𝜋. If 𝜋 −1 (\(V\) ) is open in \(X\) then since 𝜋 is surjective, we have

\[𝜋(𝜋 -1 (V )) = V\]

which is open in \(Y\) since we have assumed that 𝜋 is an open map. Hence, (1) holds. Now assume that 𝜋 is also closed in addition to being a surjective continuous map. Let \(W\) be a subset of \(Y\) . If \(W\) is closed in \(Y\) then 𝜋 −1 (\(W\) ) is closed in \(X\) by assumption of continuity of 𝜋. If 𝜋 −1 (\(W\) ) is closed in \(X\) then since 𝜋 is surjective, we have

\[𝜋(𝜋 -1 (W )) = W\]

which is closed in \(Y\) since we have assumed that 𝜋 is a closed map. Hence, (2) holds.

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Consider [0, 1] as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}\) and \(S\) 1 as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}2\) where \(\mathbb{R}2\) is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] → \(S\) 1 be the map given by 𝜋(\(t\)) = (cos(2𝜋\(t\)), sin(2𝜋\(t\))). Then, clearly, 𝜋 is a surjective continuous map. We can show that 𝜋 is also closed (to do this it helps to have defined compactness). Thus by Lemma 5.22 𝜋 is a quotient map. Note that 𝜋 is not open as 𝜋([0, \(t\))) is not open for 0 < \(t < 1\) (where we are using the fact that [0, \(t\)) is open in [0, 1] for 0 < \(t < 1)\). Let ∼ be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] given by \(s\) ∼ \(t\) if and only if 𝜋(\(s\)) = 𝜋(\(t\)), and let

\[p : [0, 1] \to [0, 1]∕∼\]

be the map given by \(p\)(\(t\)) = [\(t\)]. Then, clearly, \(p\) is a surjective continuous map. The induced bijective map

\[𝜑 : [0, 1]∕∼ \to S 1\]

given by 𝜑([\(t\)]) = 𝜋(\(t\)) is then a homeomorphism from [0, 1]∕∼ with the quotient topology induced by \(p to S\) 1 with the quotient topology induced by 𝜋. See Figure 5.6.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 41

Figure 5.6: Constructing the circle \(S\) 1 .

nodes.title: Consider as a subspace of r r r2 where r2 is given t
nodes.originalText:
Consider [0, 1] × [0, 1] as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R} = \mathbb{R}2\) where \(\mathbb{R}2\) is given the standard topology, and \(T\) 2 = \(S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}2 \times \mathbb{R}2 = \mathbb{R}4\) where \(\mathbb{R}4\) is also given the standard topology. Let 𝜋 ∶ [0, 1] × [0, 1] → \(S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 be the map given by

\[𝜋(s, t) = (cos(2𝜋s), sin(2𝜋s), cos(2𝜋t), sin(2𝜋t)).\]

Clearly, 𝜋 is a surjective continuous function. As in the previous example, we can show that 𝜋 is closed, and so, by Lemma 5.22 it is a quotient map. Let ∼ be the equivalence relation on [0, 1] × [0, 1] given by

(\(s, 0)\) ∼ (\(s, 1)\) and (0, \(t\)) ∼ (1, \(t\)),

and let \(p : [0, 1] \times [0, 1] \to ([0, 1] \times [0, 1])\)∕∼ be the map given by \(p\)(\(s, t) = [s, t]\). Then, clearly, \(p\) is a surjective continuous map. The induced bijective map

\[𝜑 : ([0, 1] \times [0, 1])∕∼ \to S 1 \times S 1\]

given by 𝜑([\(s, t])\) = 𝜋(\(s, t)\) is then a homeomorphism from ([0, 1] × [0, 1])∕∼ with the quotient topology induced by \(p to S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 with the quotient topology induced by 𝜋. The equivalence classes are then the sets

{(0, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0), (1, 1)}

(the four vertices of the square are identified),

{(\(s, 0), (s, 1)}\) and {(0, \(t\)), (1, \(t\))}

(opposing edges of the square are identified), and

{(\(s, t)}\)

for \(s, t \in (0, 1)\). See Figure 5.5.

42 5.3. Quotient spaces

nodes.title: The real projective space is the quotient space
nodes.originalText:
The real projective space is the quotient space

\[\mathbb{R}Pn = S n ∕∼\]

where \(S\) \(n\) is the \(n-sphere\) and ∼ is the equivalence relation given by \(x\) ∼ \(y\) if and only if \(x\) = ±\(y, i\).e., [\(x\)] = {\(x\), −\(x\)}. We say that \(x\) and −\(x\) are antipodal points. The topology on \(\mathbb{R}Pn\) is defined by the quotient map 𝜋 ∶ \(S\) \(n \to \mathbb{R}Pn\) given by 𝜋(\(x\)) = [\(x\)].

We end this section with an alternative description of the quotient topology.

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space let a be a set and let x a be a
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(A\) be a set, and let 𝜋 ∶ \(X \to A\) be a surjective map. The quotient topology is the only topology on \(A\) with the following universal property: for every topological space \(Y\) and every map \(f : A \to Y , f\) is continuous if and only if \(f\) ◦𝜋 ∶ \(X \to Y\) is continuous.

\(X\) \(f\) ◦𝜋 𝜋

\(A\) \(Y\) \(f\)

Proof. We follow the arguments for the proofs of Theorem 5.6 and Theorem 5.13 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the quotient topology \(\mathcal{T}\) 𝜋 has the universal property that for every topological space \(Y\) and every map \(f : A \to Y , f\) is continuous if and only if \(f\) ◦𝜋 ∶ \(X \to Y\) is continuous. Let \(A\) be given the quotient topology induced by 𝜋, and assume that \(f : A \to Y\) is continuous. By definition of the quotient topology 𝜋 −1 (\(U\) ) is open in \(X\) if and only if \(U\) is open in \(A\). Hence, 𝜋 is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 \(f\) ◦𝜋 ∶ \(X \to Y\) is continuous. Now assume that \(f\) ◦𝜋 ∶ \(X \to Y\) is continuous. Let \(V\) be an open set in \(Y\) . Since 𝜋 is a quotient map and

\[(f ◦𝜋)-1 (V ) = 𝜋 -1 (f -1 (V ))\]

is open in \(X\) by assumption of continuity of \(f\) ◦𝜋, it follows that \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) is open in \(A\). Thus \(f\) is continuous. Let \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ be a topology on \(A\) with the universal property that for every topological space \(Y\) and every map \(f : A \to Y , f\) is continuous if and only if \(f\) ◦𝜋 ∶ \(X \to Y\) is continuous. We must show that \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ = \(\mathcal{T}\) 𝜋. Let \(\mathcal{T}\) be the topology on \(X, and\) let \(A\) be given the topology \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ . First let \(Y = A\) with the quotient topology induced by \(f\) ◦𝜋. Then for \(f = id : (A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) → (\(A, \mathcal{T}\) 𝜋 ), we have

\[id ◦𝜋 = 𝜋 : (X, \mathcal{T} ) \to (A, \mathcal{T} 𝜋 )\]

which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 43

(\(X, \mathcal{T}\) ) id ◦𝜋 𝜋

(\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) (\(A, \mathcal{T}\) 𝜋 ) id

Thus any \(V \in \mathcal{T}\) 𝜋 must also be an element of \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ , and so, \(\mathcal{T}\) 𝜋 ⊆ \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ . Secondly let \(Y = A\) with \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ as its topology. Then, clearly, \(f = id : (A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) → (\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) is continu- ous. Thus by the universal property it follows that

\[id ◦𝜋 = 𝜋 : (X, \mathcal{T} ) \to (A, \mathcal{T} ′ )\]

is continuous. (\(X, \mathcal{T}\) ) id ◦𝜋 𝜋

(\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) (\(A, \mathcal{T}\) ′ ) id

Since the quotient topology induced by 𝜋 is the finest topology on \(A\) such that 𝜋 is a continuous map, cf. Lemma 5.17, it follows that \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ ⊆ \(\mathcal{T}\) 𝜋 . Hence, \(\mathcal{T}\) ′ = \(\mathcal{T}\) 𝜋 .

5.4 Exercises

Exercise 5.1 Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Show that any subspace of the form (\(a, b), i\).e., an open interval, is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb{R}\).

Exercise 5.2 Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(Y\) be a subspace of \(X\). If \(A\) is a subset of \(Y , show\) that the subspace topology on \(A\) inherited from \(Y\) is equal to the subspace topology on \(A\) inherited from \(X\).

Exercise 5.3Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and consider the set of rational numbers ℚ as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}\). Show that the subset { √ } | √ \(A= x\)∈ℚ|− 5<\(x< 5\) | of ℚ is both open and closed in ℚ.

Exercise 5.4 Let \(X = {a, b, c, d}\) be given the topology \(\mathcal{T}X = {\emptyset, {a}, {a, c, d}, {c, d}, X}, and\) let \(Y = {1, 2, 3}\) be given the topology \(\mathcal{T}Y = {\emptyset, {1}, {1, 3}, Y\) }. Find a basis for the product topology on \(X \times Y\) .

Exercise 5.5 Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces, and let \(A\) and \(B\) be subsets of \(X\) and \(Y , respectively\). Show that the topology on \(A \times B\) as a subspace of the product \(X \times Y\) is equal to the product topology on \(A \times B\) where \(A\) and \(B\) are given the subspace topology inherited from \(X\) and \(Y , respectively\).

44 5.4. Exercises

Exercise 5.6 Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. Show that the product topology is the coarsest topology on \(X \timesY\) for which both of the projection maps pr 1 ∶ \(X \timesY \to X\) and pr 2 ∶ \(X \timesY \to Y\) are continuous.

Exercise 5.7 Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two topological spaces, and let \(X \times Y\) be given the product topology. Show that if \(f : X \to Y\) is a continuous map, the subspace

\[G = {(x, y) \in X \times Y \mid y = f (x)}\]

of \(X \times Y , is\) homeomorphic to \(X\).

Exercise 5.8 Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Let

\[𝜋: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{Z}\]

be the map given by { \(x\) \(x\in\mathbb{Z}\) 𝜋(\(x\)) = \(n\) \(x \in (n - 1, n + 1), and\) \(n\) is an odd integer.

Show that the quotient topology on \(\mathbb{Z}\) induced by 𝜋 is equal to the digital line topology, cf. Exercise 4.6.

6. Topological properties

6.1 Connected spaces One of the fundamental results of calculus is the intermediate value theorem. The theorem says that for a continuous map \(f : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}\) and for a real number \(r\) between \(f\) (\(a\)) and \(f\) (\(b\)) there is a real number \(c \in [a, b]\) such that \(f\) (\(c\)) = \(r\). See Figure 6.1. From this result we can deduce that the graph of a continuous map (in this setting) is connected.

\(f\) (\(b\))

\[y = f (x)\]

Figure 6.1: The intermediate value theorem.

nodes.title: Connected space
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. A separation of \(X\) is a pair of non-empty subsets \(U\) and \(V\) that are open in \(X, disjoint\) and whose union equal \(X\). We say that \(X\) is connected if there are no separations of \(X\). Otherwise it is disconnected.

The property of being connected is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of open subsets. In other words, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic topological spaces and \(X\) is connected then so is \(Y\) .

nodes.title: Let x be the set a b c d e if we equip x with the topology
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be the set {\(a, b, c, d, e}\). If we equip \(X\) with the topology \(\mathcal{T}1 = {\emptyset, {a, b}, {a, b, c}, {a, b, d, e}, {d, e}, X}\) then it is disconnected; the pair \(U = {a, b, c}\) and \(V = {d, e}\) is a separation of \(X\) in this topology. However, if we equip \(X\) with the topology \(\mathcal{T}2 = {\emptyset, {a, b, c}, {c}, {c, d, e}, X}\) then it is connected; there are no separations of \(X\) in this topology.

46 6.1. Connected spaces

nodes.title: Let x be an indiscrete space then x is connected as there ar
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be an indiscrete space. Then \(X\) is connected as there are no separations of \(X, i\).e., there are no non-empty open subsets of \(X\) who are disjoint and whose union equal \(X\).

nodes.title: Let x be a discrete space containing two or more points then
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a discrete space containing two or more points. Then \(X\) is disconnected. Let \(p \in X\) and let \(U = {p}\) and \(V = U\) \(c = X\) ⧵ {\(p\)}. Then the pair \(U\) and \(V\) is a separation of \(X\).

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let \(X = [0, 1)\) ∪ (1, 2) be a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}\). Since \(U = [0, 1)\) is open in \(X\) (but not in \(\mathbb{R}\)) and \(V = (1, 2)\) is open \(X, U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\) and \(U\) ∪ \(V = X, they\) form a separation of \(X\). Thus \(X\) is disconnected.

In the above examples the spaces that are connected all share the property that the only subsets that are both open and closed in \(X\) are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\). Likewise, the disconnected spaces all share the property that there are non-empty proper subsets of \(X\) that are both open and closed in \(X\).

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space then x is connected if and only
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then \(X\) is connected if and only if the are no non-empty proper subsets of \(X\) that are both open and closed in \(X\).

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that \(X\) is disconnected if and only if there are non-empty proper subsets of \(X\) that are both open and closed in \(X\). Assume \(X\) is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of \(X\). Let \(U\) and \(V\) be a separation of \(X\). Thus \(U\) is open in \(X\). Since \(U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\) and \(U\) ∪ \(V = X, we\) have \(U\) \(c = X\) ⧵ \(U = V\) . Thus \(U\) \(c\) is open in \(X, and\) so, \(U\) is closed in \(X\). Hence, \(U\) is both open and closed in \(X\). Likewise, \(V\) is both open and closed in \(X\). Assume that the non-empty proper subset \(U\) of \(X\) is both open and closed in \(X\). Let \(V = U\) \(c = X\) ⧵ \(U\) . Then \(V\) is open in \(X, U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\) and \(U\) ∪ \(V = X\). Hence, the pair \(U\) and \(V\) is a separation of \(X\). Thus \(X\) is disconnected.

nodes.title: Let x be a connected space y be a topological space and let
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a connected space, \(Y\) be a topological space, and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a surjective continuous map. Then \(Y\) is connected.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that if \(f : X \to Y\) is a surjective continuous map and \(Y\) is disconnected then \(X\) is disconnected. Assume that \(Y\) is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of \(Y\) . Let the pair \(U\) and \(V\) be a separation of \(Y\) . Then \(f -1\) (\(U\) ) and \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) are non-empty subsets of \(X\) which are open in \(X\) as \(f\) is a surjective continuous map. Furthermore,

\(f -1\) (\(U\) ) ∩ \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) = \(f -1\) (\(U\) ∩ \(V\) ) = \(\emptyset\) and \(f -1\) (\(U\) ) ∪ \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) = \(f -1\) (\(U\) ∪ \(V\) ) = \(X\).

Hence, the pair \(f -1\) (\(U\) ) and \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) is a separation of \(X\). Thus \(X\) is disconnected.

A subset \(A\) of a topological space \(Z\) is connected if \(A\) is connected in the subspace topology. Thus the theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a connected space is connected, i.e., assuming \(X\) is a connected space and \(f : X \to Y\) is a continuous map then \(f\) (\(X\)) is connected in \(Y\) .

Chapter 6. Topological properties 47

We may also describe connectedness by way of the following theorem.

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space then x is connected if and only
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then \(X\) is connected if and only if every contin- uous map form \(X to a\) discrete space, with at least two points, is constant.

Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that \(X\) is disconnected if and only if there is a non-constant continuous map from \(X to a\) discrete space (with at least two points). Assume that \(X\) is disconnected. Let \(U\) be a non-empty proper subset of \(X\) that is both open and closed in \(X\). Let \(Y = {a, b}\) be given the discrete topology. The map \(f : X \to Y\) that sends \(U to a\) and \(U\) \(c = X\) ⧵ \(U to b\) is continuous and not constant. Let \(Y\) be a discrete space with at least two points. Assume that \(f : X \to Y\) is a non-constant continuous map. Then for each \(y \in f\) (\(X\)), i.e., \(y = f\) (\(x\)) for some \(x \in X, f -1\) ({\(y\)}) is a non-empty proper subset of \(X\) that is both open and closed in \(X\). Thus by Theorem 6.6, \(X\) is disconnected.

We will prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected. To prove this we will need the following two results.

nodes.title: Let x be a disconnected space with separation u and v and le
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a disconnected space with separation \(U\) and \(V , and\) let \(A\) be a connected subspace of \(X\). Then \(A \subseteq U\) or \(A \subseteq V\) .

Proof. Since \(U\) and \(V\) are open in \(X, the\) intersections \(A\) ∩ \(U\) and \(A\) ∩ \(V\) are both open in \(A\) (in the subspace topology). Furthermore, the complement of \(A\)∩\(U\) in \(A\) is equal to \(A\)∩\(V\) as \(U\) \(c = X\) ⧵\(U = V\) . Hence, \(A\) ∩ \(U\) is also closed in \(A\). Thus by Theorem 6.6, \(A\) ∩ \(U\) is either empty or all of \(A\) as \(A\) is connected. If \(A\) ∩ \(U = \emptyset\) then \(A \subseteq V\) . If \(A\) ∩ \(U = A\) then \(A \subseteq U\) .

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let a be a collection of co
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let {\(A\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be a collection of connected ⋂ ⋃ subspaces of \(X\) such that \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(A\lambda\) is non-empty. Then \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(A\lambda\) is connected.

⋃ Proof. Let \(Y = \lambda\)∈Λ \(A\lambda\) . Suppose that \(Y\) is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of \(Y\) . Let \(U\) and \(V\) be a separation of \(Y\) . We will show that this leads to a contradiction, and so, \(Y\) must be connected. ⋂ Let \(p \in \lambda\)∈Λ \(A\lambda\) . Then either \(p \in U\) or \(p \in V\) . Assume without loss of generality that \(p \in U\) . By Lemma 6.9 it follows that for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ either \(A\lambda \subseteq U\) or \(A\lambda \subseteq V\) . Since we have assumed that \(p \in U\) we must have \(A\lambda \subseteq U\) for all \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ. Thus \(Y \subseteq U\) . But this implies that \(V\) is empty, and hence, contradicts that \(U\) and \(V\) is a separation of \(Y\) . Thus \(Y\) is connected.

We can prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected.

nodes.title: Let x1 x2 xn be connected spaces then the product space x1 x
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X1 , X2\) , … , \(Xn\) be connected spaces. Then the product space \(X1 \times X2\) × ⋯ × \(Xn\) is connected.

Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two connected spaces. The general result then follows by an induction argument. Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two connected spaces. We must prove that \(X \times Y\) is connected. Since for each \(x \in X\) the subspace {\(x\)} × \(Y\) of \(X \times Y\) is homeomorphic to \(Y , it\) follows that {\(x\)} × \(Y\) is connected. Similarly, for each \(y \in Y\) the subspace \(X \times {y}\) is homeomorphic to \(X, and\) hence, \(X \times {y}\) is

48 6.1. Connected spaces

connected. Thus by Theorem 6.10 it follows that for each \(x \in X\) and each \(y \in Y\) the subspace ({\(x\)} × \(Y\) ) ∪ (\(X \times {y})\) is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is ({\(x\)} × \(Y\) ) ∩ (\(X \times {y}) = {(x, y)} \ne \emptyset\). Fix \(x0 \in X\) and let \(Ay = ({x0\) } × \(Y\) ) ∪ (\(X \times {y})\). Then for each \(y \in Y\) the subspace \(Ay\) of \(X \times Y\) is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is equal to {(\(x0 , y)}\). Hence, ⋃ by Theorem 6.10 it follows that \(y\inY\) \(Ay\) is connected as it is the union of connected spaces whose intersection in non-empty. Since ⋃ \(Ay = X \times Y , y\inY\)

it follows that \(X \times Y\) is connected.

∏ The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products, \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(X\lambda , if\) we equip the product with the ∏ product topology. If we equip \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(X\lambda\) with the box topology the statement is no longer true.

An important example of a connected space is the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology.

nodes.title: The real numbers are connected
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then \(\mathbb{R}\) is connected.

We will use the fact that the real numbers satisfy the following two properties.

(1) Every subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) that is bounded above has a least upper bound. This is known as the least upper bound property.

(2) If \(x, y \in \mathbb{R}\) with \(x < y\) then there is a real number \(z\) such that \(x < z < y\).

Proof. Assume that \(\mathbb{R}\) is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of \(\mathbb{R}\). Let \(U\) and \(V\) be a separation of \(\mathbb{R}, and\) choose \(a \in U\) and \(b \in V\) . We may assume without loss of generality that \(a < b\). Let \(A = [a, b]\) ∩ \(U\) and \(B = [a, b]\) ∩ \(V\) . Then the pair \(A\) and \(B\) is a separation of [\(a, b], with\) \(a \in A\) and \(b \in B\). Also note that \(A\) is bounded above by \(b\). Hence, by the least upper bound property \(A\) has a least upper bound; \(c = sup\) \(A\). Thus \(a \le c \le b\). We will show that \(c\) belongs neither to \(A\) nor to \(B\) thus contradicting the fact that \(A\) and \(B\) is a separation of [\(a, b]\). Assume that \(c \in B\). Since \(a \notin B\) and \(B\) is open in [\(a, b], it\) follows that there is a real number \(d\) such that \(a < d < c\) and (\(d, c] \subseteq B\). This implies that \(d\) is an upper bound of \(A\) and that \(d\) is less than the least upper bound \(c\). That is a contradiction, and so, \(c \notin B\). Now assume that \(c \in A\). Since \(A\) is open in [\(a, b]\) and \(b \notin A, there\) is a real number \(d\) such that [\(c, d) \subseteq A\). For any \(e \in (c, d)\) it follows that \(e \in A\) and \(e > c\). That is a contradiction to the fact that \(c\) is an upper bound of \(A\). Thus \(c \notin A\). Hence, \(c \notin A\) and \(c \notin B\). This is a contradiction to the fact that \(c \in [a, b]\) and that \(A\) and \(B\) is a separation of [\(a, b]\). Thus \(\mathbb{R}\) must be connected.

As an immediate consequence of Theorem 6.12, we get that open intervals of the form (\(a, b), (\)−∞, \(b\)) and (\(a\), ∞) are all connected as they are all homeomorphic to \(\mathbb{R}\) (with the standard topology). We can also show that every closed interval [\(a, b]\) is connected. Furthermore, by Theorem 6.11 and Theorem 6.12 \(\mathbb{R}n\) is a connected space.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 49

nodes.title: Generalized intermediate value theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a connected space, and let \(f : X \to \mathbb{R}\) be a continuous map where \(\mathbb{R}\) is given the standard topology. If \(a, b \in X\) and if \(r\) is a real number that lies between \(f\) (\(a\)) and \(f\) (\(b\)), there is a \(c \in X\) such that \(f\) (\(c\)) = \(r\).

Proof. Assume that \(r \notin f\) (\(X\)). We will show that this contradicts the assumption that \(X\) is connected. By the assumption that \(r \notin f\) (\(X\)), i.e., \(f\) (\(X\)) ⊆ \(\mathbb{R}\) ⧵ {\(r\)} = (−∞, \(r\)) ∪ (\(r\), ∞), we have a separation of \(X: U = f -1\) ((−∞, \(r\))) and \(V = f -1\) ((\(r\), ∞))

are disjoint non-empty open subsets of \(X\) whose union equals \(X\). Thus they are a separation of \(X\). This contradicts the assumption that \(X\) is connected. Hence, \(r \in f\) (\(X\)). In other words, there is a \(c \in X\) such that \(f\) (\(c\)) = \(r\).

We end this section with a discussion of path-connectivity.

nodes.title: Path-connected space
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(x, y \in X\). A path from \(x to y\) is a continuous map \(f : [a, b] \to X\) such that \(f\) (\(a\)) = \(x\) and \(f\) (\(b\)) = \(y\) where [\(a, b]\) is a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}\) with the standard topology. We say that \(X\) is path-connected if every pair of points of \(X\) can be joined by a path in \(X\).

The property of being path-connected is a topological property as it is completely described using the el- ements of \(X\) and its open subsets. In other words, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic topological spaces and \(X\) is path connected then so is \(Y\) .

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.originalText: Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then \(\mathbb{R}\) is path-connected as for any two points, \(p, q \in \mathbb{R}, there\) is a path from \(p to q\) in \(\mathbb{R}, e\).g., the path given by \(f\) (\(t\)) = (1 − \(t\))\(p + tq\) where \(t \in [0, 1]\).

nodes.title: For all n rn with the standard topology is path connected
nodes.originalText:
For all \(n \ge 2, \mathbb{R}n\) with the standard topology is path-connected, and so is \(\mathbb{R}n\) ⧵ {\(p\)} for each \(p \in \mathbb{R}n\) . For \(n = 1, \mathbb{R}\) ⧵ {\(p\)} is neither connected nor path-connected.

The next theorem states that path-connectedness implies connectedness. While the converse is not true in general, it is true that open subsets of \(\mathbb{R}n\) that are connected (where \(\mathbb{R}n\) is given the standard topology) are also path-connected. See [7, Proposition 12.25] for a proof of this fact.

nodes.title: Path-connectedness implies connectedness
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a path-connected space. Then \(X\) is connected.

Proof. Assume that \(X\) is path-connected but that \(X\) is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of \(X\). Let \(U\) and \(V\) be a separation of \(X\). Let \(f : [a, b] \to X\) be a path from \(x \in U to y \in V\) . Then \(f -1\) (\(U\) ) and \(f -1\) (\(V\) ) are disjoint non-empty open subsets of [\(a, b]\) whose union is equal to [\(a, b]\). Thus they are a separation of [\(a, b]\). This is a contradiction to the fact that [\(a, b]\) is a connected space.

50 6.2. Hausdorff spaces

nodes.title: The topologist’s sine curve
nodes.originalText:
The topologist’s sine curve is the subspace {( ( )) | } 1 | 0 < \(x \le 1\) ∪ {(0, \(y\)) ∣ −1 ⩽ \(y \le 1}\) \(S= x, sin\) | \(x\) | of \(\mathbb{R}2\) with the standard topology. It can be shown that \(S\) is connected but not path-connected. For a proof of this fact, see [4, pp. 156–157]. See Figure 6.2.

\(y\) \(S\)

\(x\)

−1

Figure 6.2: The topologist’s sine curve.

6.2 Hausdorff spaces A common feature that we typically want a topological space to have is the ability to separate the individual points. This is commonly referred to as separation axioms. We will in this section focus on the most common separation axiom.

nodes.title: Definition 6.19
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. We say that \(X\) is Hausdorff if for each pair of points \(x, y \in X, with\) \(x \ne y, there\) are disjoint neighborhoods \(U\) and \(V\) of \(x\) and \(y, respectively\). In other words, for each pair of distinct points \(x, y \in X\) there are open subsets \(U\) and \(V\) of \(X\) with \(x \in U\) and \(y \in V\) where \(U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\).

The property to be a Hausdorff space is completely described using the elements of \(X\) and its open subsets, and so, it is a topological property. In other words, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic topological spaces and \(X\) is Hausdorff then so is \(Y\) . To see this let \(X\) be a Hausdorff space and let \(y1\) and \(y2\) be distinct points in \(Y\) . If \(f : X \to Y\) is a homeomorphism then \(f -1\) (\(y1\) ) and \(f -1\) (\(y2\) ) are distinct points in \(X\). Since \(X\) is Hausdorff this means that there are disjoint neighborhoods \(U1\) and \(U2\) of \(f -1\) (\(y1\) ) and \(f -1\) (\(y2\) ), respectively. Thus \(f\) (\(U1\) ) and \(f\) (\(U2\) ) are disjoint neighborhoods of \(y1\) and \(y2 , respectively\). Hence, \(Y\) is Hausdorff.

nodes.title: Let x be the set a b c if we equip x with the discrete topol
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be the set {\(a, b, c}\). If we equip \(X\) with the discrete topology then \(X\) is Hausdorff since for all pairs of distinct points \(x, y \in X, the\) open subsets {\(x\)} and {\(y\)} are neighborhoods of \(x\) and \(y, respectively, and\) {\(x\)} ∩ {\(y\)} = \(\emptyset\). However, if we equip \(X\) with the topology {\(\emptyset, {a, b}, {b}, X}\) it is not Hausdorff; the only neighborhood of \(c\) is \(U = X, and\) no neighborhood \(V\) of either \(a\) or \(b\) can be disjoint from \(X\).

Chapter 6. Topological properties 51

nodes.title: Let x be a set and let and be two topologies on x if i
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a set, and let \(\mathcal{T}1\) and \(\mathcal{T}2\) be two topologies on \(X\). If \(\mathcal{T}2\) is finer than \(\mathcal{T}1\) and \(X\) equipped with \(\mathcal{T}1\) as its topology is Hausdorff then \(X\) equipped with \(\mathcal{T}2\) as its topology is also Hausdorff.

nodes.title: Every metric space is hausdorff
nodes.originalText:
Every metric space is Hausdorff.

Proof. Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space, and let \(x\) and \(y\) be two distinct points in \(X\). We must show that there are disjoint neighborhoods \(U\) and \(V\) of \(x\) and \(y, respectively\). Let \(\delta = d(x, y)\). Then \(\delta > 0\). Let \(U = B(x\); \(\delta\)∕2) and \(V = B(y\); \(\delta\)∕2) be neighborhoods of \(x\) and \(y, respectively\). Then by M3, \(U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\).

nodes.title: For all integers n the metric space rn d is hausdorff in p
nodes.originalText:
For all integers \(n \ge 1, the\) metric space (\(\mathbb{R}n , d)\) is Hausdorff. In particular, \(\mathbb{R}n\) with the standard topology is Hausdorff.

All Hausdorff spaces share the property that finite subsets are closed which is an immediate con- sequence of the following theorem.

nodes.title: Let x be a hausdorff space then for each x x the subset x of
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a Hausdorff space. Then for each \(x \in X\) the subset {\(x\)} of \(X\) is closed in \(X\).

Proof. Let \(x, y \in X\) with \(x \ne y\). Since \(X\) is Hausdorff, we have neighborhoods \(U\) and \(V\) of \(x\) and \(y, respectively, such\) that \(U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\). Then \(x \notin V\) . In other words, \(x \in V\) \(c = X\) ⧵ \(V\) . Since \(V\) is open, \(V\) \(c\) is closed in \(X\). Thus {\(x\)} ⊆ \(V\) \(c , and\) hence, \(y \notin {x}\). Hence, {\(x\)} = {\(x\)}. Thus {\(x\)} is closed in \(X\).

There are examples of topological spaces who are not Hausdorff but have the property that finite subsets are closed. One such example is the set of real numbers equipped with the cofinite topology. We have seen that the (finite) product of connected spaces is connected, cf. Theorem 6.11. The same statement holds for Hausdorff spaces, i.e., the (finite) product of Hausdorff spaces is Hausdorff.

nodes.title: Let x1 x2 xn be hausdorff spaces then the product space x1 x
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X1 , X2\) , … , \(Xn\) be Hausdorff spaces. Then the product space \(X1 \times X2\) × ⋯ × \(Xn\) is Hausdorff.

Proof. We prove the statement for two Hausdorff spaces. The general result then follows from an induction argument. Let (\(x1 , y1\) ) and (\(x2 , y2\) ) be two distinct points in \(X \times Y , i\).e., \(x1 \ne x2\) or \(y1 \ne y2\) . If \(x1 \ne x2\) there must be neighborhoods \(UX\) and \(VX\) in \(X\) of \(x1\) and \(x2 , respectively, such\) that \(UX\) ∩ \(VX = \emptyset\) as \(X\) is assumed to be Hausdorff. Then \(UX \times Y\) and \(VX \times Y\) are neighborhoods of (\(x1 , y1\) ) and (\(x2 , y2\) ), respectively, where (\(UX \timesY\) )∩(\(VX \timesY\) ) = \(\emptyset\). Similarly, if \(y1 \ne y2\) there must be neighborhoods \(UY\) and \(VY\) in \(Y\) of \(y1\) and \(y2 , respectively, such\) that \(UY\) ∩\(VY = \emptyset\) as \(Y\) is assumed to be Hausdorff. Then \(X \timesUY\) and \(X \times VY\) are neighborhoods of (\(x1 , y1\) ) and (\(x2 , y2\) ), respectively, where (\(X \times UY\) ) ∩ (\(X \times VY\) ) = \(\emptyset\). Hence, \(X \times Y\) is Hausdorff.

∏ The theorem be extended to hold for arbitrary products, \(\lambda\)∈Λ \(X\lambda , if\) we equip the product with either the product topology or the box topology.

52 6.3. Compact spaces

We end this section with a result that helps us decide whether or not a topological space is Haus- dorff.

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space then x is hausdorff if and only
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then \(X\) is Hausdorff if and only if the diagonal Δ = {(\(x, x)\) ∣ \(x \in X}\) is closed in the product space \(X \times X\).

Proof. Assume that \(X\) is a Hausdorff space. Then for any two distinct points \(x\) and \(y\) in \(X, i\).e., (\(x, y)\) ∈ Δ\(c = (X \times X)\) ⧵ Δ, there are disjoint neighborhoods \(U\) and \(V\) of \(x\) and \(y, respectively\). Thus \(U \times V\) is open in \(X \times X\) and (\(U \times V\) ) ∩ Δ = \(\emptyset, i\).e., \(U \times V\) ⊆ Δ\(c\) . Hence, by Theorem 3.10, it follows that Δ\(c\) is open in \(X \times X, and\) so, Δ is closed in \(X \times X\). Now assume that Δ is closed in the product space \(X \times X\). Then for any point (\(x, y) \in X \times X\) with \(x \ne y, i\).e., (\(x, y)\) ∈ Δ\(c , there\) is a basis element \(U \times V\) for the product topology on \(X \times X\) such that (\(x, y) \in U \times V\) ⊆ Δ\(c\) . Since \(U \times V\) ⊆ Δ\(c , we\) have \(U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\). Thus \(U\) and \(V\) are neighborhoods of \(x\) and \(y, respectively, such\) that \(U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\). Hence, \(X\) is Hausdorff.

6.3 Compact spaces In Section 6.1 we saw how the intermediate value theorem might be generalized to connected spaces. In this section we will see how the extreme value theorem may be generalized to compact spaces. The extreme value theorem says that for a continuous map \(f : [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}\) there are points \(m, M \in [a, b]\) such that \(f\) (\(m\)) ⩽ \(f\) (\(x\)) ⩽ \(f\) (\(M\)) for all \(x \in [a, b]\). See Figure 6.3.

\(y\)

\(f\) (\(M\))

\[y = f (x)\]

\(f\) (\(m\)) \(x\) \(a\) \(M\) \(m\) \(b\)

Figure 6.3: The extreme value theorem.

nodes.title: Cover of a space
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let  be a collection ⋃ of subsets of \(X\). We say that  is a cover of \(X, or\) covering of \(X\) if \(X = A\)∈ \(A\). If \(A\) is also open in \(X\) for each \(A\) ∈ , we say that  is an open cover of \(X, or\) open covering of \(X\). We say that ′ is a subcover of  if ′ is another cover of \(X\) that satisfies ′ ⊆ .

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let be a basis for the topo
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on \(X\). Then  is an open cover of \(X\). Similarly, if  is a subbasis for the topology on \(X, then\)  is an open cover of \(X\).

Chapter 6. Topological properties 53

nodes.title: Compact spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. We say that \(X\) is compact if every open cover  of \(X\) contains a finite subcover.

The property of being compact is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of the collection of open sets. In other words, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic topological spaces and \(X\) is compact then so is \(Y\) .

nodes.title: Let x be a finite topological space then x is compact as the
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a finite topological space. Then \(X\) is compact as there are only finitely many different open subsets \(A\) of \(X, and\) so, any collection covering \(X\) must necessarily be finite.

nodes.title: Let x be an indiscrete space then x is compact as the only o
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be an indiscrete space. Then \(X\) is compact as the only open covers are the collections {\(X\)} and {\(\emptyset, X}\) which are finite.

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.originalText: Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Since the open cover  = {(\(n - 1, n + 1)\) ∣ \(n \in \mathbb{Z}}, does\) not admit a finite subcover, \(\mathbb{R}\) is not compact.

nodes.title: Compact subspaces
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). We say that \(A\) is compact if \(A\) is compact in the subspace topology.

If \(A\) is a subspace of \(X, a\) collection  of subsets of \(X\) is a cover of \(A\) if the union of elements of  contains \(A\).

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let a be a subspace of x th
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). Then \(A\) is compact if and only if every cover of \(A\) by open subsets of \(X\) contains a finite subcollection that covers \(A\).

Proof. Assume that \(A\) is compact. Let  be a cover of \(A\) by open subsets of \(X\). Then the collection

\[ ′ = {A \cap U \mid U \in }\]

is an open cover of \(A\). Since \(A\) is compact there must be a finite subcover {\(A\) ∩ \(U1 , A\) ∩ \(U2\) , … , \(A\) ∩ \(Un\) } of  ′ . Hence, {\(U1 , U2\) , … , \(Un\) } is a finite subcollection of  that covers \(A\). Now assume that every cover of \(A\) by open subsets of \(X\) contains a finite subcollection that covers \(A\). Let  = {\(V\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be a cover of \(A\) by open subsets of \(A\). Hence, by definition of the subspace topology, cf. Definition 5.1, we have for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ that \(V\lambda = A\) ∩ \(U\lambda\) where \(U\lambda\) is an open subset of \(X\). Thus the collection  ′ = {\(U\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ is a cover of \(A\) by open subsets of \(X\). Then, by assumption, there must be a finite subcollection {\(U\lambda1 , U\lambda2\) , … , \(U\lambdan\) } that covers \(A\). Hence, {\(V\lambda1 , V\lambda2\) , … , \(V\lambdan\) } is a finite subcover of . Thus every cover of \(A\) by open subsets of \(A\) has a finite subcover, and so, \(A\) is compact.

The following two theorems indicates that being compact and being closed are closely related properties.

54 6.3. Compact spaces

nodes.title: Let x be a compact space and let a be a closed subset of x t
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a compact space, and let \(A\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Then \(A\) is compact.

⋃ Proof. Let  be a cover of \(A, i\).e., \(A \subseteq C\)∈ \(C, by\) open subsets of \(X\). Since \(A\) is closed in \(X, Ac = X\) ⧵ \(A\) is open in \(X\). Thus  =  ∪ {\(Ac\) } is an open cover of \(X\). Since \(X\) is compact there must be a finite subcover ′ ⊆  of \(X\). If ′ contains \(Ac , let\) ′′ = ′ ⧵ {\(Ac\) }. Then ′′ is a finite subcover of  that covers \(A\). If ′ does not contain \(Ac\) then ′ is a finite subcover of  that covers \(A\). Either way there is a finite subcover of  that covers \(A\). Thus \(A\) is compact.

nodes.title: Let x be a hausdorff space and let k be a subset of x which
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a Hausdorff space, and let \(K\) be a subset of \(X\) which is compact. Then \(K\) is closed in \(X\).

Proof. We show that \(K\) \(c = X\) ⧵ \(K\) is open in \(X\). Let \(x \in K\) \(c\) . Then for each \(y \in K\) there are neighborhoods \(Uy\) and \(Vy\) of \(x\) and \(y, respectively, such\) that \(Uy\) ∩ \(Vy = \emptyset, since\) \(X\) is assumed to be Hausdorff and \(x \ne y\). The collection  = {\(Vy\) ∣ \(y \in K}\) of open subsets of \(X\) covers \(K\) since ⋃ \(K\subseteq Vy\) . \(y\inK\)

Since \(K\) is assumed to be compact there must be a finite subcollection {\(Vy1 , Vy2\) , … , \(Vyn\) } that covers \(K\). Let \(V = Vy1\) ∪ \(Vy2\) ∪ ⋯ ∪ \(Vyn , and\) let \(U = Uy1\) ∩ \(Uy2\) ∩ ⋯ ∩ \(Uyn\) . Then \(U\) is open in \(X, x \in U\) and \(U\) ∩ \(V = \emptyset\). Furthermore, \(U \subseteq X\) ⧵ \(V \subseteq X\) ⧵ \(K = K\) \(c\) . Hence, \(K\) \(c\) is open in \(X\).

nodes.title: Let x be a compact space y a topological space and let f x y
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a compact space, \(Y\) a topological space and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a surjective continuous map. Then \(Y\) is compact.

Proof. Let  = {\(U\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be an open cover of \(Y\) . Then  = {\(f -1\) (\(U\lambda\) )}\(\lambda\)∈Λ is an open cover of \(X\). Since \(X\) is compact there must be a finite subcover ′ = {\(f -1\) (\(U\lambda1\) ), \(f -1\) (\(U\lambda2\) ), … , \(f -1\) (\(U\lambdan\) )} of . Then  ′ = {\(U\lambda1 , U\lambda2\) , … , \(U\lambdan\) } is a finite subcover of . Hence, \(Y\) is compact.

The theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a compact space is compact, i.e., assuming \(X\) is a compact space and \(f : X \to Y\) is a continuous map then \(f\) (\(X\)) is compact.

We will prove that the (finite) product of compact spaces is compact. To prove this we need the following result.

nodes.title: Tube lemma
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(Y\) be a compact space. If \(x \in X\) and \(U\) is an open set in the product space \(X \times Y\) containing {\(x\)} × \(Y , then\) there is a neighborhood \(W\) of \(x\) in \(X\) such that \(W \times Y \subseteq U\) .

The set \(W \times Y\) is often called a tube about {\(x\)} × \(Y\) . See Figure 6.4.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 55

\[Y X\timesY U\]

\(W\) \(x\) \(X\)

\[Figure 6.4: A tube about {x} \times Y .\]

Proof. As \(U\) is open in \(X \times Y\) and (\(x, y) \in {x} \times Y \subseteq U\) for all \(y \in Y , there\) is a basis element \(Wy \timesVy \subseteq U\) for the product topology on \(X \timesY\) such that (\(x, y) \in Wy \timesVy\) . The collection {\(Vy\) }\(y\inY\) is an open cover of \(Y\) . Since \(Y\) is compact there must be a finite subcover of {\(Vy\) }\(y\inY , say, {Vy1 , Vy2\) , … , \(Vyn\) }. Let \(n\) ⋂ \(W = Wyi\) . \(i=1\)

Then \(W\) is open in \(X, and\) it must contain \(x\). Clearly, \(n\) ( ) ⋃ \(W \timesY \subseteq Wyi \times Vyi \subseteq U\) . \(i=1\)

Thus {\(x\)} × \(Y \subseteq W \times Y\) and \(W \times Y \subseteq U\) .

The lemma is not true if we remove the assumption that \(Y\) is compact; the open set

\[U = {(x, y) \in \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R} \mid |xy| < 1} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}\]

does contain {0} × \(\mathbb{R}\) but does not contain any tube \(W \times \mathbb{R}\) containing {0} × \(\mathbb{R}\). (Here we have assumed that \(\mathbb{R}\) is given the standard topology, and hence, it is not compact, cf. Example 6.32.)

nodes.title: Let x1 x2 xn be compact spaces then the product space x1 x2
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X1 , X2\) , … , \(Xn\) be compact spaces. Then the product space \(X1 \times X2\) × ⋯ × \(Xn\) is compact.

Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two compact spaces. The general result then follows by an inductive argument. Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be compact spaces. Let  be an open cover of \(X \times Y\) . We must show that there is a finite subcover ′ of . For each \(x \in X, {x} \times Y\) is compact as it is homeomorphic to \(Y\) which is assumed to be compact. Thus there is a finite subcollection \(x\) of  that covers {\(x\)} × \(Y\) . Let ⋃ \(Ux = Ax\) ∈\(x\) \(Ax\) . Then \(Ux\) is open in \(X \times Y\) and contains {\(x\)} × \(Y\) . Thus by Lemma 6.38 for each \(x \in X\) there is a neighborhood \(Wx \subseteq X\) such that \(x \in Wx\) and \(Wx \times Y \subseteq Ux\) . Furthermore, \(x\) covers \(Wx \times Y\) . Now let \(x \in X\) vary. The collection {\(Wx\) }\(x\inX\) is then an open cover of \(X\). Since \(X\) is compact, there must be a finite subcover {\(Wx1 , Wx2\) , … , \(Wxn\) } of {\(Wx\) }\(x\inX\) . For each 1 ⩽ \(i \le n\) the subspace

56 6.3. Compact spaces

\(Wxi \times Y\) is covered by the finite subcollection \(xi\) of . Hence,

\(n\) ⋃ \(X\timesY = Wx\) \(i \times Y\) \(i=1\) ⋃\(n\) is covered by the subcollection ′ = \(i=1\) \(xi\) of . Thus \(X \times Y\) is compact.

The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products of compact spaces if we equip the product with the product topology. This is known as Tychonoff’s theorem. It is a deep result whose proof uses several original ideas. If we equip the product with the box topology the statement is no longer true. See, e.g., [4, §37] for a discussion and proof of Tychonoff’s theorem.

We have already seen that the real line (with the standard topology) is not compact, cf. Exam- ple 6.32. The next theorem states that all closed intervals of the real line are compact.

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then every closed interval [\(a, b]\) in \(\mathbb{R}\) is compact.

Proof. Let  = {\(U\lambda\) }\(\lambda\)∈Λ be a cover of [\(a, b]\) by open subsets of \(\mathbb{R}, and\) let

\(S = {x \in [a, b]\) ∣ [\(a, x]\) is covered by a finite subcollection of }.

Note that \(S\) is bounded above by \(b\). Since \(a \in U\lambda\) for some \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ, the singleton {\(U\lambda\) } is a finite subcollection of  that covers [\(a, a] = {a}\). Hence, \(S\) is non-empty and it is bounded above. Thus \(S\) has a least upper bound; \(c = sup\) \(S\). Clearly, \(a \le c \le b\). We will show that \(c \in S\). The result follows if we can also show that \(c = b\). Choose \(\lambda\)′ ∈ Λ with \(c \in U\lambda\)′ . Since \(U\lambda\)′ is open in \(\mathbb{R}\) there is a real number \(\epsilon > 0\) such that (\(c - \epsilon, c + \epsilon) \subseteq U\lambda\)′ . Moreover, there is an \(x \in S\) such that \(c - \epsilon < x, and\) so, by definition of \(S\) there is a finite subcollection ⋃ {\(U\lambda1 , U\lambda2\) , … , \(U\lambdan\) } of  such that [\(a, x] \subseteq ni=1\) \(U\lambdai\) . Furthermore, [\(x, c] \subseteq U\lambda\)′ . Thus [\(a, c] = [a, x]\) ∪ [\(x, c]\) is covered by the finite subcollection {\(U\lambda1 , U\lambda2\) , … , \(U\lambdan , U\lambda\)′ } of . Hence, \(c \in S\). We now show that \(c = b\). Assume that \(c < b\). Then there must be a \(y \in [a, b]\) such that \(c < y < c + \epsilon\). Thus [\(a, y]\) is covered by the subcollection {\(U\lambda1 , U\lambda2\) , … , \(U\lambdan , U\lambda\)′ } of  such that \(y \in S\). This is a contradiction of the fact that \(c\) is an upper bound. Hence, \(c = b\).

In order to state and prove the Heine–Borel theorem we need the following definition.

nodes.title: Bounded subsets
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, d)\) be a metric space, and let \(A\) be a subset of \(X\). We say that \(A\) is bounded if there is an \(M \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \(d\)(\(a1 , a2\) ) ⩽ \(M\) for all \(a1 , a2 \in A\).

Equivalently, we may say that a subset \(A\) of a metric space (\(X, d)\) is bounded if there is a \(K \in \mathbb{R}\) and \(x \in X\) such that \(d\)(\(a, x) \le K\) for all \(a \in A\). In particular, this means that a subset of \(\mathbb{R}n\) equipped with the Euclidean metric is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius centered at the origin.

nodes.title: Heine–Borel
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}n\) be given the (Euclidean) metric topology and the Eu- clidean metric. A subset \(A\) of \(\mathbb{R}n\) is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

Chapter 6. Topological properties 57

Proof. Assume that \(A\) is compact. By Theorem 6.22, \(\mathbb{R}n\) is Hausdorff, and so, by Theorem 6.36 \(A\) is closed in \(\mathbb{R}n\) . We must show that \(A\) is also bounded. Let  = {\(B\)(0; \(n\)) ∣ \(n \in \mathbb{Z}+ }, i\).e., a collection of open balls centered at the origin in \(\mathbb{R}n\) . Then  is a cover of \(A\) by open subsets of \(\mathbb{R}n\) . Since \(A\) is compact there must be a finite subcollection of  that covers \(A\). Thus there is an \(N \in \mathbb{Z}+ such\) that \(A \subseteq B(0\); \(N\)). Hence, for all \(a1 , a2 \in A\) we have \(d\)(\(a1 , a2\) ) < 2\(N\). Thus \(A\) is bounded. Now assume that \(A\) is bounded and closed in \(\mathbb{R}n\) . Let \(p = (p1 , p2\) , … , \(pn\) ) ∈ \(A\). Since \(A\) is bounded there is an \(M \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \(d\)(\(a1 , a2\) ) ⩽ \(M\) for all \(a1 , a2 \in A\). Then \(A\) is contained in the product space \(P = [p1 - M, p1 + M] \times [p2 - M, p2 + M]\) × ⋯ × [\(pn - M, pn + M]\)

which by Theorem 6.40 and Theorem 6.39 is compact. Since \(A\) is closed in \(\mathbb{R}n\) and a subset of \(P , it\) follows by extending Theorem 6.35 to our setting that \(A\) is compact. Specifically, let  be a cover of \(A\) by open subsets of \(\mathbb{R}n\) . Since \(A\) is closed in \(\mathbb{R}n , Ac = \mathbb{R}n\) ⧵ \(A\) is open in \(\mathbb{R}n\) . Thus

\[ =  \cup {Ac }\]

is an open cover of \(\mathbb{R}n , and\) thus it is also an open cover of \(P\) in the subspace topology. Since \(P\) is compact there must be a finite subcover of  that covers \(P\) . This implies that there is a finite subcover of  that covers \(A\). Thus \(A\) is compact.

An immediate consequence of Theorem 6.42 is that \(S\) \(n\) considered as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}n+1\) is com- pact, and hence, that the torus \(T\) 2 = \(S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 is compact. We end this section with a proof of the generalized extreme value theorem.

nodes.title: Generalized extreme value theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a compact space, and let \(f : X \to \mathbb{R}\) be a continuous map where \(\mathbb{R}\) is given the standard topology. Then there are \(m, M \in X\) such that \(f\) (\(m\)) ⩽ \(f\) (\(x\)) ⩽ \(f\) (\(M\)) for all \(x \in X\).

Proof. By Theorem 6.37, \(f\) (\(X\)) is compact. We must show that \(f\) (\(X\)) contains its supremum and its infimum. If it does, then by setting \(f\) (\(m\)) = inf \(f\) (\(X\)) and \(f\) (\(M\)) = sup \(f\) (\(X\)) the theorem follows. We prove that \(f\) (\(X\)) contains its supremum. The proof for the infimum is similar. Since \(f\) (\(X\)) is compact, it is closed and bounded by Theorem 6.42. In particular, \(f\) (\(X\)) is bounded above. Hence, the set \(f\) (\(X\)) has a least upper bound; \(s = sup\) \(f\) (\(X\)). Thus \(p \le s\) for all \(p \in f\) (\(X\)). We must show that \(s \in f\) (\(X\)). Assume that \(s \notin f\) (\(X\)). Since \(f\) (\(X\)) is closed, i.e., \(f\) (\(X\))\(c = \mathbb{R}\)⧵\(f\) (\(X\)) is open, it follows that there is a real number \(\epsilon > 0\) such that (\(s - \epsilon, s + \epsilon) \subseteq f\) (\(X\))\(c , and\) so,

\[(s - \epsilon, s + \epsilon) \cap f (X) = \emptyset.\]

Hence, there is a real number \(y\) such that \(y\) is an upper bound of \(f\) (\(X\)) and \(s - \epsilon < y < s\). This is a contradiction to the fact that \(s\) is the least upper bound of \(f\) (\(X\)). Hence, \(s = sup\) \(f\) (\(X\)) ∈ \(f\) (\(X\)).

58 6.4. Exercises

6.4 Exercises

Exercise 6.1 Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. Show that if \(f : X \to Y\) is surjective continuous map and \(X\) is path-connected then \(Y\) is also path-connected.

We say that a subset \(A\) of a topological space \(X\) is path-connected if \(A\) is path-connected in the subspace topology. Hence, the previous exercise may be extended to saying that the continuous image of a path-connected space is path-connected.

Exercise 6.2 Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A \subseteq B \subseteq A\) be subspaces of \(X\).

(a) Show that if \(A\) is connected then so is \(B\).

(b) Show that [\(a, b), (a, b], [a, b], (\)−∞, \(b\)] and [\(a\), ∞) are all connected spaces when considered as subspaces of \(\mathbb{R}\) with the standard topology.

Exercise 6.3 Let \(X\) be a topological space, and consider \(I = [0, 1]\) as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}\) where \(\mathbb{R}\) is given the standard topology. Furthermore, let the cone on \(X\) be quotient space \(CX = X\timesI\)∕∼, where ∼ is the equivalence relation on the product space \(X \times I\) given by (\(x, 0)\) ∼ (\(x\)′ , 0) for all \(x, x\)′ ∈ \(X\). Show that \(CX\) is path-connected.

Exercise 6.4 Let \(X\) be a Hausdorff space, and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). Show that \(A\) is Hausdorff.

Exercise 6.5 Let \(X\) be an infinite set with the cofinite topology.

(a) Show that \(X\) is compact.

(b) Show that any subset of \(X\) is compact.

Exercise 6.6 Let \(X\) be a compact space, and let \(Y\) be a Hausdorff space. Furthermore, let \(f : X \to Y\) be a continuous map.

(a) Show that \(f\) is a closed map.

(b) Show that if \(f\) is a surjective continuous map, then \(f\) is a quotient map.

(c) Show that if \(f\) a bijective continuous map, then \(f\) is a homeomorphism.

(d) Show that \(f\) is proper, i.e., for each subset \(K\) of \(Y\) that is compact the preimage \(f -1\) (\(K\)) is compact.

Exercise 6.7 Show that the surface of the cube centered at the origin,

\[C = {(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}3 \mid max{|x|, |y|, |z|} = 1},\]

and the 2-sphere, { } |√ \(S\) 2 = (\(x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}3\) | \(x2 + y2 + z2 = 1 , |\)

Chapter 6. Topological properties 59

are homeomorphic where they are both considered to be subspaces of \(\mathbb{R}3\) with the standard topology.

Exercise 6.8 Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A1\) .\(A2\) , … , \(An\) be subspaces of \(X\) each of which is compact. Show that \(n\) ⋃ \(Ai\) \(i=1\)

is compact.

7. The fundamental group

7.1 Homotopy of paths The topological properties we have discussed so far do not help us to distinguish between, e.g., the 2-sphere \(S\) 2 and the torus \(T\) 2 = \(S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 . In order to prove that \(S\) 2 and \(T\) 2 are not topologically equivalent, i.e., \(S\) 2 ≇ \(T\) 2 , we need new properties and new techniques. A such property is that of simple connectedness. A path-connected space \(X\) is, roughly speaking, simply connected if every closed curve in \(X\) can be shrunk to a point in \(X\). The 2-sphere \(S\) 2 is simply connected as any closed curve on \(S\) 2 can be shrunk to a point in \(S\) 2 . On the other hand, the torus \(T\) 2 is not simply connected as there are closed curves which cannot be shrunk to a point in \(T\) 2 . See Figure 7.1. To be simply connected is a topological property. Hence, if \(X\) and \(Y\) are homeomorphic topological spaces and one of them is simply connected then so is the other. We can express that a path connected space is simply connected by saying that its fundamental group is trivial. In particular, if \(X \cong Y\) then their fundamental groups are isomorphic. We will show that the fundamental group of \(S\) 2 is not isomorphic to the fundamental group of \(T\) 2 . Hence, \(S\) 2 ≇ \(T\) 2 . Computing fundamental groups provides us with more information than just that of being simply connected.

Figure 7.1: The 2-sphere \(S\) 2 is simply connected while the torus \(T\) 2 = \(S\) 1 ×\(S\) 1 is not simply connected.

In order to define the fundamental group of a topological space, we will need the concept of ho- motopy (of paths).

nodes.title: Homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two topological spaces, and let \(f0 , f1 : X \to Y\) be two continuous maps. Furthermore, let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers with the standard topology, \(I = [0, 1]\) be a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}, and\) let \(X \times I\) be given the product topology. We say that \(f0\) is homotopic to \(f1 , written\) \(f0\) ≃ \(f1 , if\) there is a continuous map

\[H: X \timesI \to Y\]

such that \(H\)(\(x, 0) = f0\) (\(x\)) and \(H\)(\(x, 1) = f1\) (\(x\)) for all \(x \in X\). The map \(H\) is called a homotopy between \(f0\) and \(f1\) . If \(f0\) ≃ \(f1\) and \(f1\) is a constant map, we say that \(f0\) is nullhomotopic.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 61

We can think of a homotopy as a continuous one-parameter family {\(H\)|\(X\times{t}\) }\(t\inI\) of continuous maps from \(X to Y\) . See Figure 7.2.

\(f1\) \(I\) \(Y\) \(t\) \(H\)

\(X\)

Figure 7.2: A homotopy from \(f0 to f1\) .

nodes.title: Straight-line homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then any two continu- ous maps \(f0 , f1 : X \to \mathbb{R}n , where\) \(\mathbb{R}n\) is given the standard topology, are homotopic. The map \(H : X \times I \to \mathbb{R}n\) given by

\[H(x, t) = (1 - t)f0 (x) + tf1 (x).\]

for all \(x \in X\) and all \(t \in I = [0, 1]\) is a homotopy between \(f0\) and \(f1\) . We call it the straight-line homotopy as it deforms for each \(x \in X\) the point \(f0\) (\(x\)) to the point \(f1\) (\(x\)) along the straight-line segment between them. In particular, any continuous map \(f : X \to \mathbb{R}n\) is homotopic to the constant map \(c : X \to \mathbb{R}n\) given by \(c\)(\(x\)) = 0 for \(x \in X\). Hence, any continuous map into \(\mathbb{R}n\) is nullhomotopic.

We will need the following lemma to prove that ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps between a topological space \(X\) and a topological space \(Y\) .

nodes.title: Pasting lemma
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X = A\) ∪ \(B\) be a topological space, where \(A\) and \(B\) are closed in \(X\). Furthermore, let \(Y\) be a topological space, and assume that \(f : A \to Y\) and \(g : B \to Y\) are continuous maps. If \(f\) (\(x\)) = \(g\)(\(x\)) for all \(x \in A\) ∩ \(B, then\) the map ℎ ∶ \(X \to Y\) given by { \(f\) (\(x\)) \(x \in A\) ℎ(\(x\)) = \(g\)(\(x\)) \(x \in B\) is continuous.

Proof. We will prove that the preimage of a closed subset of \(Y\) under ℎ is closed in \(X\). Let \(C\) be a closed subset of \(Y\) . Then ℎ−1 (\(C\)) = \(f -1\) (\(C\)) ∪ \(g -1\) (\(C\)). Since \(f\) is continuous, it follows that \(f -1\) (\(C\)) is closed in \(A, and\) so, it is closed in \(X\). Similarly, \(g -1\) (\(C\)) is closed in \(B, and\) so, it is closed in \(X\). Hence, ℎ−1 (\(C\)) = \(f -1\) (\(C\)) ∪ \(g -1\) (\(C\))

62 7.1. Homotopy of paths

is closed in \(X\) as it is the union of two closed subsets of \(X\). Thus ℎ is continuous.

nodes.title: The relation is an equivalence relation on the set of all co
nodes.originalText:
The relation ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps from a topological space \(X to a topological\) space \(Y\) .

Proof. We must show that ≃ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. For any continuous map \(f : X \to Y\) there is a homotopy \(H : X \times I \to Y\) given by \(H\)(\(x, t) = f\) (\(x\)) for all \(x \in X\) and all \(t \in I\). Then \(H\)(\(x, 0) = f\) (\(x\)) and \(H\)(\(x, 1) = f\) (\(x\)) for all \(x \in X\). Hence, \(f\) ≃ \(f\) . Thus ≃ is reflexive. Let \(f0 : X \to Y\) and \(f1 : X \to Y\) be two continuous maps, and let \(H : X \times I \to Y\) be a homotopy between them. Then \(H : X \times I \to Y\) given by \(H\)(\(x, t) = H(x, 1 - t)\) is a homotopy between \(f1\) and \(f0\) . Hence, \(f0\) ≃ \(f1\) implies \(f1\) ≃ \(f0\) . Thus ≃ is symmetric. To show that ≃ is transitive, we must show that if \(f0\) ≃ \(f1\) and \(f1\) ≃ \(f2 , then\) \(f0\) ≃ \(f2\) . Let \(H1 : X \times I \to Y\) be a homotopy from \(f0 to f1 , and\) let \(H2 : X \times I \to Y\) be a homotopy from \(f1 to f2\) . Then there is a map \(H1\) ∗ \(H2 : X \times I \to Y\) given by { \(H1\) (\(x, 2t)\) 0 ⩽ \(t \le 1\)∕2 (\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(\(x, t) = H2\) (\(x, 2t - 1)\) 1∕2 ⩽ \(t \le 1\). It is continuous for all \(t \in I = [0, 1]\) by Lemma 7.3 since \(X \times [0, 1\)∕2] and \(X \times [1\)∕2, 1] are closed subsets of \(X \times I\) whose union is equal to \(X \times I, and\) \(H1\) (\(x, 1) = H2\) (\(x, 0)\) for all \(x \in X\). Hence, \(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) is a homotopy from \(f0 to f2\) . Thus ≃ is transitive.

nodes.title: Homotopy classes
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces, and let \(C\)(\(X, Y\) ) be the set of continuous maps from \(X to Y\) . The homotopy classes in \(C\)(\(X, Y\) ) are the equivalence classes under the relation ≃. We write [\(f\) ] for the homotopy class of \(f \in C(X, Y\) ), i.e.,

\[[f ] = {g \in C(X, Y ) \mid f ≃ g},\]

and we write [\(X, Y\) ] for the set of homotopy classes of continuous maps from \(X to Y , i\).e., [\(X, Y\) ] = \(C\)(\(X, Y\) )∕≃.

If we can connect two points \(x\) and \(y\) in a topological space \(X\) by a path, i.e., a continuous map \(f : [a, b] \to X\) such that \(f\) (\(a\)) = \(x\) and \(f\) (\(b\)) = \(y, cf\). Definition 6.14, we say that they lie in the same path component. Specifically, the relation ∼\(p\) given by \(x\) ∼\(p\) \(y\) if and only if there is a path from \(x to y\) in \(X, is\) an equivalence relation on \(X\). We say that an equivalence class in \(X\) under ∼\(p\) is a path component. We write 𝜋0 (\(X\)) for the set of path components of \(X\). Note that 𝜋0 (\(X\)) consists of only one element if and only if \(X\) is path-connected.

nodes.title: Let x x0 and y be topological spaces then the continuous map
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X = {x0\) } and \(Y\) be topological spaces. Then the continuous maps \(f : X \to Y\) correspond to the points \(f\) (\(x0\) ) = \(y \in Y\) . Two continuous maps \(f0 , f1 : X \to Y\) are homotopic if and only if there is a path from \(y0 = f0\) (\(x0\) ) to \(y1 = f1\) (\(x0\) ). Hence, the homotopy classes of continuous maps from \(X to Y\) correspond to the path components of \(Y , and\) [\(X, Y\) ] corresponds to 𝜋0 (\(Y\) ).

We are particularly interested in the case where we have homotopies between paths in a topological space \(X\). For simplicity, let all paths be continuous maps from \(I = [0, 1] to X\).

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 63

nodes.title: Path homotopy
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(x0 , x1 \in X\). We say that two paths \(f , g : I \to X\) in \(X\) from \(x0 to x1\) are path homotopic, written \(f\) ≃\(p\) \(g, if\) there is a continuous map \(H : I \times I \to X\) such that

\(H\)(\(s, 0) = f\) (\(s\)) and \(H\)(\(s, 1) = g(s)\)

for all \(s \in I, and\)

\(H\)(0, \(t\)) = \(x0\) and \(H\)(1, \(t\)) = \(x1\)

for all \(t \in I\). We call \(H\) a path homotopy from \(f to g\).

Then they are path homotopic: the straight-line homotopy \(H : I \times I \to \mathbb{R}n\) given by \(H\)(\(s, t) = (1 - t)f\) (\(s\)) + \(tg\)(\(s\)) for all \(s \in I\) and all \(t \in I\) is a path homotopy from \(f to g\). See Figure 7.3.
