nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let x0 x1 x then the relati
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(x0 , x1 \in X\). Then the relation ≃\(p\) is an equivalence relation on the set of all paths from \(x0 to x1\) in \(X\).

Proof. The result follows by proving that the homotopies constructed in the proof of Theorem 7.4 when applied to paths are path homotopies. Let \(f : I \to X\) be a path from \(x0 to x1\) . Then the constant homotopy \(H : I \times I \to X\) given by

\[H(s, t) = f (s)\]

is path homotopy from \(f to f : H(s, 0) = f\) (\(s\)) and \(H\)(\(s, 1) = f\) (\(s\)) for all \(s \in I, and\) \(H\)(0, \(t\)) = \(f\) (0) = \(x0\) and \(H\)(1, \(t\)) = \(f\) (1) = \(x1\) for all \(t \in I\). Assume that \(H : I \times I \to X\) is a path homotopy between paths \(f : I \to X\) and \(g : I \to X\) from \(x0 to x1\) . Then the homotopy \(H : I \times I \to X\) given by

\[H(s, t) = H(s, 1 - t)\]

is a path homotopy from \(g to f : H(s, 0) = H(s, 1) = g(s)\) and \(H\)(\(s, 1) = H(s, 0) = f\) (\(s\)) for all \(s \in I, and\) \(H\)(0, \(t\)) = \(H\)(0, 1 − \(t\)) = \(x0\) and \(H\)(1, \(t\)) = \(H\)(1, 1 − \(t\)) = \(x1\) for all \(t \in I\). Finally, assume that \(f0 : I \to X, f1 : I \to X\) and \(f2 : I \to X\) are paths from \(x0 to x1 , and\) that \(H1 : I \times I \to X\) is a path homotopy from \(f0 to f1\) and that \(H2 : I \times I \to X\) is a path homotopy from \(f1 to f2\) . Then the homotopy \(H1\) ∗ \(H2 : I \times I \to X\) given by { \(H1\) (\(s, 2t)\) 0 ⩽ \(t \le 1\)∕2 (\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(\(s, t) = H2\) (\(s, 2t - 1)\) 1∕2 ⩽ \(t \le 1\)

is a path homotopy from \(f0 to f2 : (H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(\(s, 0) = H1\) (\(s, 0) = f0\) (\(s\)) and (\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(\(s, 1) = H2\) (\(s, 1) = f2\) (\(s\)) for all \(s \in I, and\) (\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(0, \(t\)) = \(x0\) and (\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(1, \(t\)) = \(x1\) for all \(t \in I\).

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 65

nodes.title: Path homotopy classes
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(x0 , x1 \in X\). If \(f : I \to X\) is a path from \(x0 to x1 , we\) write [\(f\) ] for its path homotopy class, i.e.,

[\(f\) ] = {\(g : I \to X\) ∣ \(g\) is a path from \(x0 to x1\) and \(f\) ≃\(p\) \(g\)}.

The fundamental group of a topological space \(X\) with basepoint \(x0\) is, as a set, the set of path homotopy classes of paths from \(x0 to x0 , i\).e., the set of path homotopy classes of loops based at \(x0\) . The group structure of the fundamental group is derived from the following product.

nodes.title: Product of paths
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(x0 , x1 , x2 \in X\). If \(f : I \to X\) is a path from \(x0 to x1 , and\) \(g : I \to X\) is a path from \(x1 to x2 , we\) define the product of \(f\) and \(g\) as the path \(f\) ∗ \(g : I \to X\) from \(x0 to x2\) given by { \(f\) (2\(s\)) 0 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)∕2 (\(f\) ∗ \(g\))(\(s\)) = \(g\)(2\(s - 1)\) 1∕2 ⩽ \(s \le 1\).

Note that the map \(f\) ∗ \(g\) is well-defined and continuous by Lemma 7.3. Furthermore, as (\(f\) ∗ \(g\))(0) = \(f\) (0) = \(x0\) and (\(f\) ∗ \(g\))(1) = \(g\)(1) = \(x2 , f\) ∗ \(g\) is a path from \(x0 to x2\) . See Figure 7.5.

\(X\)

nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(x0 , x1 , x2 \in X\). If \(f : I \to X\) is a path from \(x0 to x1\) and \(g : I \to X\) is a path from \(x1 to x2 , then\) the product \(f\) ∗ \(g\) induces a well-defined operation on path homotopy classes given by

\[[f ] ∗ [g] = [f ∗ g].\]

Proof. Let \(f\) ′ ∶ \(I \to X\) be a path from \(x0 to x1 , and\) let \(g\) ′ ∶ \(I \to X\) be a path from \(x1 to x2\) . Fur- thermore, let \(H1 : I \times I \to X\) be a path homotopy from \(f to f\) ′ , and let \(H2 : I \times I \to X\) be a path homotopy from \(g to g\) ′ . Then the map \(H1\) ∗ \(H2 : I \times I \to X\) given by { \(H1\) (2\(s, t)\) 0 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)∕2 (\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(\(s, t) = H2\) (2\(s - 1, t)\) 1∕2 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)

66 7.1. Homotopy of paths

is a path homotopy from \(f\) ∗ \(g to f\) ′ ∗ \(g\) ′ : it is well-defined since \(H1\) (1, \(t\)) = \(x1 = H2\) (0, \(t\)) for all \(t \in I\) and it is continuous by Lemma 7.3, and

(\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(\(s, 0) = (f\) ∗ \(g\))(\(s\)) and (\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(\(s, 1) = (f\) ′ ∗ \(g\) ′ )(\(s\)) for all \(s \in I, (H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(0, \(t\)) = \(H1\) (0, \(t\)) = \(x0\) and (\(H1\) ∗ \(H2\) )(1, \(t\)) = \(H2\) (1, \(t\)) = \(x2\) for all \(t \in I\).

\[Hence, f ∗ g induces a well-defined operation on path homotopy classes given by [f ] ∗ [g] = [f ∗ g].\]

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space then the product of paths has t
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then the product of paths, ∗, has the following properties on the set of path homotopy classes in \(X\).

Associativity Let \(x0 , x1 , x2\) and \(x3\) be points in \(X\). If \(f0 : I \to X\) is a path from \(x0 to x1 , f1 : I \to X\) is a path from \(x1 to x2 , and\) \(f2 : I \to X\) is a path from \(x2 to x3 , then\)

\[([f0 ] ∗ [f1 ]) ∗ [f2 ] = [f0 ] ∗ ([f1 ] ∗ [f2 ]).\]

Left and right units For \(x \in X, let\) \(cx : I \to X\) denote the constant path at \(x, given\) by \(cx\) (\(s\)) = \(x\) for all \(s \in I\). If \(f : I \to X\) is a path from \(x0 to x1\) then

\[[cx0 ] ∗ [f ] = [f ] = [f ] ∗ [cx1 ].\]

Inverse If \(f : I \to X\) is a path from \(x0 to x1 , let\) \(f : I \to X\) be the reverse path from \(x1 to x0 , given\) by \(f\) (\(s\)) = \(f\) (1 − \(s\)) for all \(s \in I\). Then

[\(f\) ] ∗ [\(f\) ] = [\(cx0\) ] and [\(f\) ] ∗ [\(f\) ] = [\(cx1\) ].

Proof. We prove that ∗ is an associative operation on the set of path homotopy classes in \(X\). 7.2 Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group The set of path homotopy classes of paths in a topological space \(X\) does not form a group under the product of paths as the product of two path homotopy classes is not always defined. However, such a product is defined if we restrict to paths that begin and end at some fixed point \(x0\) . This is the fundamental group of \(X\) with basepoint \(x0\) . We say that the ordered pair (\(X, x0\) ) is a based space (sometimes also called a pointed space).

nodes.title: The fundamental group
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ) be a based space. A path \(f : I \to X\) from \(x0 to x0\) is called a loop in \(X\) based at \(x0\) . Let

𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) = {[\(f\) ] ∣ \(f\) is a loop in \(X\) based at \(x0\) }

be the set of path homotopy classes of loops in \(X\) based at \(x0\) . We say that 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) is the fundamental group of \(X\) based at \(x0\) .

From the definition it is clear that 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) depends only on the path component of \(X\) that contains \(x0\) . The fundamental group does not provide us with any information about the rest of \(X\). As the notation suggests, the fundamental group is sometimes also referred to as the first homotopy group of \(X\). In fact, there are groups 𝜋\(n\) (\(X, x0\) ) for all \(n \in \mathbb{Z}\)+ . These groups are part of a subject called homotopy theory.

If we restrict Theorem 7.14 to the case where all paths are loops in \(X\) based at \(x0 , we\) get the following result.

nodes.title: Let x x0 be a based space then the fundamental group x x0
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ) be a based space. Then the fundamental group 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) of \(X\) based at \(x0\) is, in fact, a group with product of paths, ∗, as its binary operation. The identity element \(e\) is equal to the path homotopy class of the constant path at \(x0 , e = [cx0\) ], and the inverse of [\(f\) ] is [\(f\) ]−1 = [\(f\) ] where \(f\) is the reverse path of \(f\) .

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 71

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.originalText: Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. If \(f : I \to \mathbb{R}\) is a loop in \(\mathbb{R}\) based at 0, by Example 7.2 we know that \(f\) is path homotopic to the constant path at 0. Thus 𝜋1 (\(\mathbb{R}, 0)\) is the trivial group, i.e., 𝜋1 (\(\mathbb{R}, 0) = {e}\). In general, 𝜋1 (\(\mathbb{R}n , 0)\) is the trivial group, where \(\mathbb{R}n\) is given the standard topology. We will often denote the trivial group as simply 0.

nodes.title: Let x x0 be a based space where x is a discrete space then
nodes.originalText: Let (\(X, x0\) ) be a based space where \(X\) is a discrete space. Then 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) = 0 for all \(x0 \in X\) as the path component containing \(x0\) consists of only one element, namely \(x0\) .

nodes.title: Let x be a path connected space and let x0 x1 x then x x0
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a path-connected space, and let \(x0 , x1 \in X\). Then 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) is isomorphic to 𝜋1 (\(X, x1\) ).

Proof. Let 𝛼 ∶ \(I \to X\) be a path from \(x0 to x1\) . If \(f : I \to X\) is a loop in \(X\) based \(x0 , then\) 𝛼 ∗ \(f\) ∗ 𝛼 ̂ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(X, x1\) ) by is a loop in \(X\) based at \(x1\) . We define the map 𝛼

\[̂([f ]) = [𝛼]-1 ∗ [f ] ∗ [𝛼] 𝛼\]

where we have used the fact that [ 𝛼 ] = [𝛼]−1 . It is a well-defined map as ∗ is well-defined, and moreover, it only depends on the path homotopy class of 𝛼. We will show that 𝛼 ̂ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(X, x1\) ) is an isomorphism. We will first show that 𝛼 ̂ is a homomorphism, and then show that it is bijective. Since

\[̂([f ] ∗ [g]) = [𝛼]-1 ∗ [f ] ∗ [g] ∗ [𝛼] 𝛼 = ([𝛼]-1 ∗ [f ] ∗ [𝛼]) ∗ ([𝛼]-1 ∗ [g] ∗ [𝛼]) =𝛼 ̂([f ]) ∗ 𝛼 ̂([g]),\]

for all [\(f\) ], [\(g\)] ∈ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ), it follows that 𝛼 ̂ is a homomorphism. To show that 𝛼 ̂ is bijective, and hence, an isomorphism, we show that 𝛼 ̂ has an inverse. Let 𝛽 = 𝛼, i.e., 𝛽 is the reverse path of 𝛼. Since ̂ 𝛽([ℎ]) = [𝛽]−1 ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛽] = [𝛼] ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛼]−1 𝛽([ℎ])) = [𝛼]−1 ∗ ([𝛽]−1 ∗ [ℎ] ∗ [𝛽]) ∗ [𝛼] = [ℎ] ̂( ̂ 𝛼

In light of Theorem 7.19 it might be tempting to simply speak of the fundamental group of a path-connected space \(X, and\) thus leave out any mention of the basepoint. However, while 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) ≅ 𝜋1 (\(X, x1\) ) for any two points \(x0\) and \(x1\) in \(X, the\) isomorphism may depend on the path from \(x0 to x1\) . It can be shown that the isomorphism of 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) with 𝜋1 (\(X, x1\) ) for a path-connected space \(X\) is independent of path if and only if the fundamental group is abelian.

72 7.2. Definition and elementary properties of the fundamental group

nodes.title: Simply connected spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a path-connected space. We say that \(X\) is simply connected if 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) is the trivial group for some \(x0 \in X, and\) hence, for all \(x0 \in X\).

By Example 7.17, we get that \(\mathbb{R}n\) is simply connected where \(\mathbb{R}n\) is given the standard topology. We shall later see that the circle \(S\) 1 and the torus \(T\) 2 = \(S\) 1 × \(S\) 1 are two examples of non-simply connected spaces.

nodes.title: Based maps
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ) and (\(Y , y0\) ) be based spaces. A based map ℎ ∶ (\(X, x0\) ) → (\(Y , y0\) ) is a continuous map ℎ ∶ \(X \to Y\) such that ℎ(\(x0\) ) = \(y0\) .

Let (\(X, x0\) ) and (\(Y , y0\) ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (\(X, x0\) ) → (\(Y , y0\) ) be a based map. If \(f : I \to X\) is a loop in \(X\) based at \(x0 , the\) composition ℎ◦\(f : I \to Y\) is a loop in \(Y\) based at \(y0\) . This leads to a map from 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) to 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ).

nodes.title: Homomorphisms induced by based maps
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ) and (\(Y , y0\) ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (\(X, x0\) ) → (\(Y , y0\) ) be a based map. The map

\[h∗ : 𝜋1 (X, x0 ) \to 𝜋1 (Y , y0 )\]

given by ℎ∗ ([\(f\) ]) = [ℎ◦\(f\) ] is called the homomorphism induced by ℎ.

nodes.title: Let x x0 and y y0 be based spaces and let h x x0 y y0 be a
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ) and (\(Y , y0\) ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (\(X, x0\) ) → (\(Y , y0\) ) be a based map. The map ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ) given by ℎ∗ ([\(f\) ]) = [ℎ◦\(f\) ] is a homomorphism.

Proof. We need to show that for two loops \(f : I \to X\) and \(g : I \to X\) in \(X\) based at \(x0 , the\) identity

\[h∗ ([f ] ∗ [g]) = h∗ ([f ]) ∗ h∗ ([g])\]

holds in 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ). The left-hand side is the path homotopy class of ℎ◦(\(f\) ∗ \(g\)) and the right-hand side is the path homotopy class of (ℎ◦\(f\) ) ∗ (ℎ◦\(g\)). By definition, we have { (ℎ◦\(f\) )(2\(s\)) 0 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)∕2 ((ℎ◦\(f\) ) ∗ (ℎ◦\(g\)))(\(s\)) = (ℎ◦\(g\))(2\(s - 1)\) 1∕2 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)

and so { ℎ(\(f\) (2\(s\))) 0 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)∕2 (ℎ◦(\(f\) ∗ \(g\)))(\(s\)) = ((ℎ◦\(f\) ) ∗ (ℎ◦\(g\)))(\(s\)) = ℎ(\(g\)(2\(s - 1))\) 1∕2 ⩽ \(s \le 1\). Hence, ℎ◦(\(f\) ∗ \(g\)) = (ℎ◦\(f\) ) ∗ (ℎ◦\(g\)). Thus ℎ∗ ([\(f\) ] ∗ [\(g\)]) = ℎ∗ ([\(f\) ]) ∗ ℎ∗ ([\(g\)]) holds in 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ).

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 73

nodes.title: Functoriality
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ), (\(Y , y0\) ) and (\(Z, z0\) ) be based spaces, and let ℎ1 ∶ (\(X, x0\) ) → (\(Y , y0\) ) and ℎ2 ∶ (\(Y , y0\) ) → (\(Z, z0\) ) be based maps. Then

\[(h2 ◦h1 )∗ = (h2 )∗ ◦(h1 )∗ .\]

If id\(X : X \to X\) is the identity map, then (id\(X\) )∗ is the identity automorphism of 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ).

The rule sending a based space (\(X, x0\) ) to its fundamental group 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) and a based map ℎ ∶ (\(X, x0\) ) → (\(Y , y0\) ) to its induced homomorphism ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ) is a functor from the category of based spaces and based maps to the category of groups and (group) homomorphisms.

Proof. Assume that \(f : I \to X\) is a loop in \(X\) based at \(x0\) . Then

\[(h2 ◦h1 )∗ ([f ]) = [(h2 ◦h1 )◦f ]\]

and ( ) (ℎ2 )∗ (ℎ1 )∗ ([\(f\) ]) = [ℎ2 ◦(ℎ1 ◦\(f\) )].

Since composition of continuous maps is associative, i.e., (ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )◦\(f\) = ℎ2 ◦(ℎ1 ◦\(f\) ), these path homo- topy classes must be equal. Hence, (ℎ2 ◦ℎ1 )∗ = (ℎ2 )∗ ◦(ℎ1 )∗ . If id\(X : X \to X\) is the identity map, then id\(X\) ◦\(f = f , and\) so, (id\(X\) )∗ ([\(f\) ]) = [id\(X\) ◦\(f\) ] = [\(f\) ]. Hence, (id\(X\) )∗ is the identity automorphism of 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ).

The following corollary of Theorem 7.24 tells us that the fundamental group is a topological in- variant of based spaces.

nodes.title: Corollary 7.25
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ) and (\(Y , y0\) ) be based spaces. If ℎ ∶ \(X \to Y\) is a homeomorphism such that ℎ(\(x0\) ) = \(y0 , then\) ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ) is an isomorphism.

The fundamental group thus allows us to distinguish between two based spaces provided that their funda- mental groups are not isomorphic. By Example 7.17, we know that 𝜋1 (\(\mathbb{R}, 0) = 0\) and 𝜋1 (\(\mathbb{R}2 , 0) = 0\). However, by Example 6.16, we know that \(\mathbb{R}\) and \(\mathbb{R}2\) are not homeomorphic. Hence, it is possible for two non-homeomorphic based spaces to have isomorphic fundamental groups.

Proof. We must show that ℎ∗ is bijective. Let \(k : Y \to X\) be the inverse homemorphism of ℎ such that \(k\)(\(y0\) ) = \(x0\) . Then, by Theorem 7.24, \(k\)∗ ◦ℎ∗ = (\(k\)◦ℎ)∗ = (id\(X\) )∗ , and ℎ∗ ◦\(k\)∗ = (ℎ◦\(k\))∗ = (id\(Y\) )∗ . Hence, \(k\)∗ = (ℎ∗ )−1 .

To compute the fundamental group of a product space, we may use the following theorem.

nodes.title: Let x x0 and y y0 be based spaces then xy x0 y0 is isomorp
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ) and (\(Y , y0\) ) be based spaces. Then 𝜋1 (\(X\timesY , (x0 , y0\) )) is isomorphic to the direct product 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) × 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ).

74 7.3. Homotopy type

Proof. Let pr 1 ∶ \(X \times Y \to X\) and pr 2 ∶ \(X \times Y \to Y\) be the projection maps onto the first and second factor, respectively. Define the map 𝜑 ∶ 𝜋1 (\(X \times Y , (x0 , y0\) )) → 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) × 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ) by ( ) ( ) 𝜑([ℎ]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) = [pr 1 ◦ℎ], [pr 2 ◦ℎ] .

Note that 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) × 𝜋1 (\(Y , yo\) ) is a group with binary operation given by

\[([f ], [g]) ∗ ([f ′ ], [g ′ ]) = ([f ] ∗ [f ′ ], [g] ∗ [g ′ ])\]

for [\(f\) ], [\(f\) ′ ] ∈ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) and [\(g\)], [\(g\) ′ ] ∈ 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ). We will show that 𝜑 is an isomorphism. It is a homomorphism since (pr 1 )∗ and (pr 2 )∗ are homo- morphisms: ( ) 𝜑([ℎ] ∗ [\(k\)]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ] ∗ [\(k\)]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ] ∗ [\(k\)]) ( ) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 1 )∗ ([\(k\)]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 2 )∗ ([\(k\)]) ( ) ( ) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) ∗ (pr 1 )∗ ([\(k\)]), (pr 2 )∗ ([\(k\)]) = 𝜑([ℎ]) ∗ 𝜑([\(k\)])

for [ℎ], [\(k\)] ∈ 𝜋1 (\(X \times Y , (x0 , y0\) )). Let \(f : I \to X\) be a loop in \(X\) based at \(x0 , and\) let \(g : I \to Y\) be a loop in \(Y\) based at \(y0\) . Define the map ℎ ∶ \(I \to X \times Y\) by

\[h(s) = (f (s), g(s)) .\]

Then ℎ is a loop in \(X \times Y\) based at (\(x0 , y0\) ), and ( ) ( ) 𝜑([ℎ]) = (pr 1 )∗ ([ℎ]), (pr 2 )∗ ([ℎ]) = [pr 1 ◦ℎ], [pr 2 ◦ℎ] = ([\(f\) ], [\(g\)]).

Hence, 𝜑 is surjective. To show that 𝜑 is injective, assume that 𝜑([ℎ]) = 𝜑([\(k\)]) for [ℎ], [\(k\)] ∈ 𝜋1 (\(X \times Y , (x0 , y0\) )). Then there is a path homotopy \(H1 : I \timesI \to X\) from pr 1 ◦ℎ to pr 1 ◦\(k\) and a path homotopy \(H2 : I \times I \to Y\) from pr 2 ◦ℎ to pr 2 ◦\(k\). Define the map \(H : I \times I \to X \times Y\) by ( ) \(H\)(\(s, t) = H1\) (\(s, t), H2\) (\(s, t)\) .

Then \(H\) is a path homotopy from ℎ = (pr 1 ◦ℎ, pr 2 ◦ℎ) to \(k = (pr\) 1 ◦\(k, pr\) 2 ◦\(k\)). Hence, [ℎ] = [\(k\)], and so, 𝜑 is injective. Thus 𝜑 is an isomorphism.

7.3 Homotopy type We will see in this section how we may obtain information about the fundamental group by way of homotopy types. This allows us to compute the fundamental group of a topological space by computing the fundamental group of some other topological space that is hopefully easier to work with.

nodes.title: Let x x0 and y y0 be based spaces and let h x x0 y y0 and
nodes.originalText:
Let (\(X, x0\) ) and (\(Y , y0\) ) be based spaces, and let ℎ ∶ (\(X, x0\) ) → (\(Y , y0\) ) and \(k : (X, x0\) ) → (\(Y , y0\) ) be based maps. If there is a homotopy \(H : X \times I \to Y\) from ℎ to \(k\) such that \(H\)(\(x0 , t) = y0\) for all \(t \in I, then\) the homomorphisms ℎ∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ) and \(k\)∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ) induced by ℎ and \(k, respectively, are\) equal.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 75

Proof. If \(f : I \to X\) is a loop in \(X\) based at \(x0 , then\) \(H\)◦(\(f \times id) : I \times I \to Y\) is a homotopy from ℎ◦\(f to k\)◦\(f\) where id ∶ \(I \to I\) is the identity map of \(I\). Since (\(H\)◦(\(f \times id))(s, 0) = (\)ℎ◦\(f\) )(\(s\)) and (\(H\)◦(\(f \times id))(s, 1) = (k\)◦\(f\) )(\(s\)) for all \(s \in I, (H\)◦(\(f \times id))(0, t) = y0\) and (\(H\)◦(\(f \times id))(1, t) = y0\) for all \(t \in I, H\)◦(\(f \times id)\) is a path homotopy from ℎ◦\(f to k\)◦\(f\) . Hence, ℎ∗ ([\(f\) ]) = [ℎ◦\(f\) ] = [\(k\)◦\(f\) ] = \(k\)∗ ([\(f\) ]). Thus ℎ∗ = \(k\)∗ .

nodes.title: Retractions
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). We say that a continuous map \(r : X \to A\) is a retraction of \(X\) onto \(A\) if \(r\)(\(a\)) = \(a\) for each \(a \in A\). If there is a retraction of \(X\) onto \(A, we\) say that \(A\) is a retract of \(X\).

nodes.title: The circle s is a retract of r2 where r2 is given the st
nodes.originalText:
The circle, \(S\) 1 , is a retract of \(\mathbb{R}2\) ⧵ {0} where \(\mathbb{R}2\) is given the standard topology. Specifically, the continuous map \(r : \mathbb{R}2\) ⧵ {0} → \(S\) 1 given by \(x\) \(r\)(\(x\)) = ‖\(x\)‖ √ where ‖\(x\)‖ = \(x21 + x22 , is\) a retraction of \(\mathbb{R}2\) ⧵ {0} onto \(S\) 1 .

If \(i : A \to X\) is the inclusion map, then we can express that \(r : X \to A\) is a retraction of \(X\) onto \(A\) by the following commutative diagram. \(i\) \(A\) \(X\) \(r\) id\(A\) \(A\)

Hence, for a retraction \(r, we\) have \(r\)◦\(i = idA\) where id\(A : A \to A\) is the identity map of \(A\).

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let a be a subspace of x if
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). If \(x0 \in A\) and \(A\) is a retract of \(X, then\) the homomorphism \(i\)∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(A, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) induced by the inclusion map \(i : A \to X\) is a monomorphism.

Proof. Let \(r : X \to A\) be a retraction of \(X\) onto \(A\). Then, by Theorem 7.24 and the definition of retraction, we have the following commutative diagram \(i\)∗ 𝜋1 (\(A, x0\) ) 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) \(r\)∗ (id\(A\) )∗ 𝜋1 (\(A, x0\) )

where id\(A\) is the identity map of \(A\). By Theorem 7.24, we know that (id\(A\) )∗ is an isomorphism. Thus \(r\)∗ ◦\(i\)∗ is an isomorphism, and hence, also bijective. This implies that \(i\)∗ is injective. Hence, \(i\)∗ is a monomorphism.

The homomorphism induced by the inclusion of \(A\) into \(X\) is an isomorphism if \(A\) is a deformation retract.

76 7.3. Homotopy type

nodes.title: Deformation retracts
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). A homotopy \(H: X \timesI \to X\) is called a deformation retraction of \(X\) onto \(A\) if \(H\)(\(x, 0) = x\) and \(H\)(\(x, 1) \in A\) for all \(x \in X, and\) \(H\)(\(a, t) = a\) for all \(a \in A\) and all \(t \in I\). We say that \(A\) is a deformation retract of \(X\).

The map \(r : X \to A\) defined by \(r\)(\(x\)) = \(H\)(\(x, 1)\) is a retraction of \(X\) onto \(A\). Thus \(H\) is a homotopy between the identity map of \(X\) and the map \(i\)◦\(r\) where \(i : A \to X\) is the inclusion map. Note that some authors call what we have defined as a deformation retract, a strong deformation retract. In this terminology a homotopy \(H : X \times I \to Y\) is a deformation retract of \(X\) onto \(A\) if \(H\)(\(x, 0) = x\) and \(H\)(\(x, 1) \in A\) for all \(x \in X, and\) \(H\)(\(a, 1) = a\) for all \(a \in A\).

Every deformation retract is also a retract but the converse is not true in general.

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let a x then a a is retract
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(a \in X\). Then \(A = {a}\) is retract of \(X\) with retraction \(r : X \to A\) given by \(r\)(\(x\)) = \(a\). If \(A\) is a deformation retract of \(X, then\) \(X\) must be path-connected: the deformation retraction gives that there is a path from each \(x \in X to a\). However, there are path-connected spaces that do not deformation retract onto a point.

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let a be a subspace of x if
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). If \(x0 \in A\) and \(A\) is a deformation retract of \(X, then\) the homomorphism \(i\)∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(A, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) induced by the inclusion map \(i : A \to X\) is an isomorphism.

Proof. Let \(H : X \times I \to X\) be a deformation retraction of \(X\) onto \(A, and\) let a retraction \(r : X \to A\) of \(X\) onto \(A\) be given by \(r\)(\(x\)) = \(H\)(\(x, 1)\). By Lemma 7.27 and Theorem 7.24, we have \(i\)∗ ◦\(r\)∗ = (id\(X\) )∗ where id\(X\) is the identity map of \(X\). Since \(r\)◦\(i = idA , we\) have \(r\)∗ ◦\(i\)∗ = (id\(A\) )∗ where id\(A\) is the identity map of \(A\). Thus both \(r\)∗ ◦\(i\)∗ and \(i\)∗ ◦\(r\)∗ are isomorphisms. Hence, \(i\)∗ ∶ 𝜋1 (\(A, x0\) ) → 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) is an isomorphism with \(r\)∗ as its inverse.

We may generalize the notion of deformation retraction. This leads us to the notion of homotopy equivalence.

nodes.title: Homotopy equivalences
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. If \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to X\) are continuous maps such that \(g\)◦\(f\) is homotopic to the identity map of \(X, idX , and\) \(f\) ◦\(g\) is homotopic to the identity map of \(Y , idY , we\) say that \(f\) and \(g\) are homotopy equivalences. We say that each of \(f\) and \(g\) is a homotopy inverse of the other.

Clearly, every homeomorphism is also a homotopy equivalence. The converse is not true in general.

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then for any \(p \in \mathbb{R}, the\) map \(f : \mathbb{R} \to {p}\) given by \(f\) (\(x\)) = \(p\) is a homotopy equivalence with homotopy inverse \(g : {p} \to \mathbb{R}\) given by \(g\)(\(p\)) = \(p\). It is clear that \(f\) is not a homeomorphism. In fact, there are no homeomorphisms between \(\mathbb{R}\) and {\(p\)}.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 77

nodes.title: Homotopy types
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. We say that \(X\) and \(Y\) have the same homotopy type if there is a homotopy equivalence \(f : X \to Y\) .

It is common to also refer to topological spaces of the same homotopy type as homotopy equivalent. We end this section with a result showing that topological spaces of the same homotopy type have isomorphic fundamental groups. To prove the result we will need the following lemma.

78 7.3. Homotopy type

By Lemma 7.3, \(H\) ′ is continuous. Since

\[H ′ (s, 0) = H(h(s), 1) = g(h(s))\]

and ⎧𝛼(4\(s\)) 0 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)∕4 ′ ⎪ \(H\) (\(s, 1)\) = ⎨\(H\)(ℎ(4\(s - 1), 0)\) 1∕4 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)∕2 ⎪𝛼(2\(s - 1)\) 1∕2 ⩽ \(s \le 1\) ⎩ ⎧𝛼(4\(s\)) 0 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)∕4 ⎪ = ⎨\(f\) (ℎ(4\(s - 1))\) 1∕4 ⩽ \(s \le 1\)∕2 ⎪𝛼(2\(s - 1)\) 1∕2 ⩽ \(s \le 1\) ⎩

for all \(s \in I, and\) \(H\) ′ (0, \(t\)) = \(H\)(\(x0 , 1) = y1\) and \(H\) ′ (1, \(t\)) = \(H\)(\(x0 , 1) = y1 , it\) follows that \(H\) ′ is a path homotopy from \(g\)◦ℎ to 𝛼 ∗ (\(f\) ◦ℎ) ∗ 𝛼. Thus [\(g\)◦ℎ] = [𝛼 ∗ (\(f\) ◦ℎ) ∗ 𝛼]. This completes the proof.

nodes.title: Let x and y be topological spaces and let f x y be a homotop
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces, and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a homotopy equivalence such that \(f\) (\(x0\) ) = \(y0\) . Then

\[f∗ : 𝜋1 (X, x0 ) \to 𝜋1 (Y , y0 )\]

is an isomorphism.

Proof. Let \(g : Y \to X\) be a homotopy inverse of \(f\) with \(g\)(\(y0\) ) = \(x1 , and\) let \(f\) (\(x1\) ) = \(y1\) . By assump- tion, we have \(g\)◦\(f\) ≃ id\(X\) and \(f\) ◦\(g\) ≃ id\(Y\) where id\(X : X \to X\) and id\(Y : Y \to Y\) are the identity maps of \(X\) and \(Y , respectively\). Thus, by Lemma 7.37, there is a path 𝛼 ∶ \(I \to X\) in \(X\) from \(x0 to x1\) and a path 𝛽 ∶ \(I \to Y\) from \(y0 to y1\) such that

\[(g◦(fx0 ))∗ = g∗ ◦(fx0 )∗ = 𝛼 ̂◦(idX )∗ = 𝛼 ̂\]

and

\[̂ Y )∗ = 𝛽̂ ((fx1 )◦g)∗ = (fx1 )∗ ◦g∗ = 𝛽◦(id\]

where we have specified the basepoint for the homomorphisms induced by \(f\) . In other words, the following two diagrams commute. (id\(X\) )∗ \(g\)∗ 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) 𝜋1 (\(X, x0\) ) 𝜋1 (\(Y , y0\) ) 𝜋1 (\(X, x1\) ) 𝛼 ̂ (\(fx1\) )∗ \(g\)∗ ◦(\(fx0\) )∗ 𝛽̂ 𝜋1 (\(X, x1\) ) 𝜋1 (\(Y , y1\) )

Since 𝛼̂ and 𝛽̂ are both isomorphisms, it follows by commutativity of the diagram on the left that \(g\)∗ is an epimorphism, and by commutativity of the diagram on the right that \(g\)∗ is a monomorphism. Hence, \(g\)∗ is an isomorphism. Since (\(fx0\) )∗ = (\(g\)∗ )−1 ◦̂ 𝛼 it follows that (\(fx0\) )∗ is an isomorphism.

Chapter 7. The fundamental group 79

We can use Theorem 7.38 to help us determine the fundamental group of certain topological spaces, and as a tool to determine that certain topological spaces are not of the same homotopy type, and hence, not homeo- morphic.

7.4 Exercises

Exercise 7.1 Let \(X, Y\) and \(Z\) be topological spaces. Show that if \(f : X \to Y\) is homotopic to \(f\) ′ ∶ \(X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) is homotopic to \(g\) ′ ∶ \(Y \to Z, then\) \(g\)◦\(f : X \to Z\) is homotopic to \(g\) ′ ◦\(f\) ′ ∶ \(X \to Z\).

Exercise 7.2 Let \(X\) be a topological space. Show that \(X\) is path-connected if and only if every two constant maps \(c1 : X \to X\) and \(c2 : X \to X\) are homotopic.

Exercise 7.3 Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(x0 , x1\) and \(x2\) be points in \(X\). Show that if 𝛼 ∶ \(I \to X\) is a path in \(X\) from \(x0 to x1\) , 𝛽 ∶ \(I \to X\) is a path in \(X\) from \(x1 to x2\) and 𝛾 = 𝛼 ∗ 𝛽, ̂ 𝛼. then 𝛾̂ = 𝛽◦̂

Exercise 7.4 Let \(S\) 2 be the 2-sphere considered to be a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}3\) with the standard topology. Show that 𝜋1 (\(S\) 2 ⧵ {(0, 0, 1)}, \(s\)) is the trivial group where \(s = (0, 0\), −1).

Exercise 7.5 Let \(X\) be a Hausdorff space, and let \(A\) be a subspace of \(X\). Show that if \(A\) is a retract of \(X, then\) \(A\) is a closed subset of \(X\).

Exercise 7.6 Show that the relation of homotopy equivalence is an equivalence relation on any set of topological spaces.

A topological space \(X\) is said to be contractible if the identity map of \(X, idX : X \to X, is\) nullho- motopic.

Exercise 7.7 Show that a topological space \(X\) is contractible if and only if \(X\) has the homotopy type of a point, i.e., a topological space consisting of one point.

Exercise 7.8 Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(S\) 1 be the unit circle considered as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}2\) where \(\mathbb{R}2\) is given the standard topology. Show that if \(f : X \to S\) 1 is a continuous map that is not surjective, then \(f\) is nullhomotopic.

8. The fundamental group of the circle

8.1 Covering spaces The notion of a covering space is a helpful tool for determining fundamental groups. Specifically, we will compute the fundamental group of the circle by first establishing that the map \(p : \mathbb{R} \to S\) 1 , given by \(p\)(\(t\)) = (cos(2𝜋\(t\)), sin(2𝜋\(t\))),

is a covering map.

nodes.title: Covering spaces
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText:
Let \(B\) and \(E\) be topological spaces. We say that a surjective continuous map \(p : E \to B\) is a covering map if for each point \(b \in B\) there is a neighborhood \(U\) such that \(p-1\) (\(U\) ) is a disjoint union of open subsets \(V\lambda\) of \(E\) where \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ, ⨆ \(p-1\) (\(U\) ) = \(V\lambda , \lambda\)∈Λ

and \(p\)|\(V\lambda : V\lambda \to U\) is a homeomorphism for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ. We refer to \(E\) as a covering space of \(B\).

A covering map \(p : E \to B\) is sometimes also referred to as a covering projection. Note that \(B\) is often referred to as the base space of the covering map and that \(E\) is often referred to as the total space of the covering map. An open subset \(U\) of \(B\) satisfying the condition that ⨆ \(p-1\) (\(U\) ) = \(V\lambda , where\) \(V\lambda\) is an open subset of \(E, \lambda\)∈Λ

such that \(p\)|\(V\lambda : V\lambda \to U\) is a homeomorphism for each \(\lambda\) ∈ Λ is said to be evenly covered by \(p\). See Figure 8.1. For each \(b \in B, we\) often refer to the preimage \(p-1\) (\(b\)) of {\(b\)} under \(p\) as the fiber over \(b\). Note also that the fiber over \(b\) is a discrete subspace of \(E\); each \(V\lambda\) is an open subset of \(E\) and intersects the fiber over \(b\) in a single point, and so, this point is open in the fiber over \(b\). Finally, we note that a covering map is a special case of a fiber bundle with discrete fibers.

nodes.title: Let b and e be topological spaces if p e b is a homeomorphis
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText: Let \(B\) and \(E\) be topological spaces. If \(p : E \to B\) is a homeomorphism, then \(p\) is also a covering map.

nodes.title: Let x be a topological space and let d be a discrete space t
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, and let \(D\) be a discrete space. Then the projection map onto the first factor, pr 1 ∶ \(X \times D \to X, where\) \(X \times D\) is given the product topology, is a covering map. In particular, \(X\) is evenly covered by pr 1 .

Chapter 8. The fundamental group of the circle 81

nodes.title: Local homeomorphisms
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText: Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces. A continuous map \(f : X \to Y\) is said to be a local homeomorphism if for each \(x \in X\) there is a neighborhood \(U\) such that \(f\) (\(U\) ) is open in \(Y\) and \(f\) |\(U : U \to f\) (\(U\) ) is a homeomorphism where \(U\) and \(f\) (\(U\) ) are both given the subspace topology from \(X\) and \(Y , respectively\).

nodes.title: Let x and y be topological spaces and let f x y be a homeomo
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText:
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be topological spaces, and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a homeomorphism. Then \(f\) is also a local homeomorphism.

Local homeomorphisms preserve local topological properties such as local connectedness and local path- connectedness. In other words, if \(f : X \to Y\) is a local homeomorphism and, say, \(X\) is locally connected then so is \(Y\) . A topological space \(X\) is locally (path) connected at \(x \in X\) if for each neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) there is a (path) connected neighborhood \(V\) of \(x\) such that \(V \subseteq U\) . We say that \(X\) is locally (path) connected if \(X\) is locally (path) connected at each \(x \in X\). Note that a (path) connected space need not be locally (path) connected. Clearly, a covering map is also a local homeomorphism. Thus if \(p : E \to B\) is a covering map, then the total space and the base space are locally equivalent but they can differ globally.

The following theorem provides an important example of a covering space that we will use later on to compute the fundamental group of the circle.

nodes.title: Let r be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
nodes.sectionTitle: The fundamental group of the circle
nodes.originalText:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and consider the circle \(S\) 1 as a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}2\) where \(\mathbb{R}2\) is given the standard topology. Then the map \(p : \mathbb{R} \to S\) 1 given by \(p\)(\(t\)) = (cos(2𝜋\(t\)), sin(2𝜋\(t\))) is a covering map.

82 8.1. Covering spaces

Proof. Clearly, \(p\) is continuous and surjective. Let \(U = S\) 1 ⧵ {(−1, 0)} and \(V = S\) 1 ⧵ {(1, 0)}. Then both \(U\) and \(V\) are open in \(S\) 1 , and their union is equal to \(S\) 1 . We want to show that they are evenly covered by \(p\). The preimage of \(U\) under \(p\) is the disjoint union of subsets \(V\lambda = (\lambda - 1\)∕2, \(\lambda + 1\)∕2) of \(\mathbb{R}\) for \(\lambda \in \mathbb{Z}\); ⨆ ⨆( 1 1 ) \(p-1\) (\(U\) ) = \(V\lambda = \lambda\) − ,\(\lambda\) + . \(\lambda\in\mathbb{Z}\) \(\lambda\in\mathbb{Z}\) 2 2

Similarly, the preimage of \(V\) under \(p\) is the disjoint union of subsets \(W\lambda = (\lambda, \lambda + 1)\) of \(\mathbb{R}\) for \(\lambda \in \mathbb{Z}\); ⨆ ⨆ \(p-1\) (\(V\) ) = \(W\lambda = (\lambda, \lambda + 1)\). \(\lambda\in\mathbb{Z}\) \(\lambda\in\mathbb{Z}\)

Since \(p\) is continuous and open, cf. [5, Lemma 14.33], and \(V\lambda\) and \(W\lambda\) are open subsets of \(\mathbb{R}, it\) follows that \(p\)|\(V\lambda : V\lambda \to U\) and \(p\)|\(W\lambda : W\lambda \to V\) are open maps for each \(\lambda \in \mathbb{Z}\). Moreover, they are both also bijective for each \(\lambda \in \mathbb{Z}\). Hence, by Theorem 5.21 they are both homeomorphisms. Thus \(p : \mathbb{R} \to S\) 1 is a covering map.

The covering map \(p\) from Theorem 8.6 is illustrated in Figure 8.2. Theorem 8.6 also provides an example of a universal covering space since \(\mathbb{R}\) is simply connected. It is universal in the following sense: for any covering map \(q : E \to S\) 1 where we assume that \(E\) is path-connected, there is a covering map \(r : \mathbb{R} \to E\) such that \(p = q\)◦\(r\). \(r\) \(\mathbb{R}\) \(E\)
